## Symptom

After a proxy restart, the monitor's proxy pane permanently shows "No API requests logged yet" although the proxy writes all dual logs correctly. Observed live 2026-06-10 ~00:54.

## Diagnostic data (verified by Opus, live evidence — verify independently in the code)

The per-project marker file `src/logs/.proxy_session_<sid>` (and its /tmp twin `/tmp/.monitor_cc_proxy_<sid>`) was deleted and never re-created. Read-side: `parse_proxy_log_forwarded` (src/proxy_display/forwarded_parser.py) resolves the log_id via marker line 2; marker missing → falls back to `log_id = <sid>` → nonexistent file → graceful empty return → pane empty forever.

Evidenced race timeline in `src/claude_proxy_start.sh`:

1. 00:50 — old proxy session (log_id `opus_monitor_cc_1781033215`) writes its last log entry.
2. 00:51 — NEW proxy session starts (log_id `opus_monitor_cc_1781045499`). Marker-write guard (lines ~73-86): old marker's referenced `_forwarded` log has mtime < 60s → `MARKER_IS_STALE=false` → new session does NOT overwrite the marker. Marker keeps the OLD log_id. (The guard exists to protect parallel sessions of the same project, e.g. hi-test — see comments lines 69-72.)
3. 00:54 — OLD session exits. Its `cleanup()` trap (lines ~257-281): marker line 2 == its own log_id (the new session never claimed it) → `rm -f` marker. Same pattern deletes the /tmp marker (guarded by port, line 1).
4. Nothing ever re-writes either marker (write happens only once at session start). Both markers gone → pane blind; worker spawns also lose proxy discovery (workers spawned during the gap run unproxied).

Both markers were restored MANUALLY (port 8081, log_id `opus_monitor_cc_1781045499`) as an immediate mitigation. The lifecycle itself is still broken for the next restart that falls into the 60s window.

Additional context: the mtime<60s guard itself replaced an earlier port-listening check that had false-positives from port reuse by unrelated mitmdump instances (comment lines 71-72). Do not regress to that.

Fix the marker lifecycle in `src/claude_proxy_start.sh` so that the per-project marker (and the /tmp marker) reliably points at a LIVE proxy session:

- across restarts (the race above must be impossible), AND
- without breaking the parallel-session protection (a second session for the same project must not clobber the marker of a live first session), AND

Issue #13 geht es darum, dass beim Start einer neuen Proxy-Version automatisch alle Dual-Logs aufgeräumt werden, die noch von einer früheren Proxy-Version stammen. Das Problem hinter dem Wunsch: das aktuelle Cleanup ist rein zeitbasiert (`cleanup_old_jsonl`, ~30s Retention) und versionsblind. Dadurch können nach einem Fix-Neustart noch alte Log-Einträge der Vor-Fix-Version im Pane auftauchen, und man zieht den falschen Schluss, der Fix habe nicht funktioniert.

Was dafür gebraucht wird, laut Issue: eine Proxy-Versions-Markierung auf den Log-Dateien bzw. -Einträgen, eine Erkennung des Versionswechsels beim Proxy-Start, und dann das Löschen der Logs älterer Versionen bei einem erkannten Wechsel.

Mein Verständnis der Aufgabe: Wir bauen einen versionsbewussten Janitor-Mechanismus, der die heutige zeitbasierte Logik ergänzt oder ersetzt, sodass beim Proxy-Versionswechsel gezielt die Alt-Version-Logs verschwinden.

📋 **PLAN — Step 3: Gap Analysis (Findings + 2 Entscheidungen)**

**Was in den Hash gehört.** Das Start-Skript kopiert beim Start genau zwei Dinge als „lebenden" Proxy: `src/proxy_addon.py` (nur ein 31-Zeilen-Import-Shim) und das gesamte `src/proxy/`-Package (21 Dateien, ~3.800 Zeilen — `addon.py`, `logging.py`, `rules.py`, alle `strip_*`-Module). Die ganze Logik liegt im Package. Die natürliche Definition der „Proxy-Version" ist also der Content-Hash über exakt diese live-kopierte Menge — das, was wirklich läuft. Das Start-Skript selbst würde ich bewusst **nicht** mit hashen: wenn wir mal nur die Janitor-Logik im Skript anpassen, soll das keinen Purge auslösen. Version = was Requests verarbeitet, nicht die Orchestrierung drumherum.

**Der Live-Session-Schutz — dein Punkt.** Genau richtig, die live geschriebenen v1-Logs einer noch laufenden Session dürfen beim v2-Start nicht mitgelöscht werden. Die saubere Lösung: der Versions-Purge löscht nur Dual-Logs, die **nicht frisch beschrieben** wurden — konkret eine mtime-Schranke (z.B. nichts löschen, was in den letzten 120s angefasst wurde). Eine aktiv laufende Session schreibt bei jeder Nachricht in ihre `_forwarded`-/`_original`-Dateien, die mtime bleibt also frisch → geschützt. Eine beendete Alt-Session hat stale mtime → wird gepurgt. Das ist genau die Technik, die das Skript für seinen Marker-Staleness-Check schon nutzt (dort 60s-Fenster), rein in Bash, ohne Prozess-Mapping. Den Schwellwert würde ich großzügig setzen, damit eine kurz pausierende Session nicht erwischt wird.

Ehrlich zum Restrisiko: eine Session, die *komplett* länger als die Schranke idle ist, aber technisch noch läuft, würde bei einem gleichzeitigen Anders-Versions-Start trotzdem gepurgt. Das ist ein sehr enger Fall (idle Alt-Session + paralleler Neu-Versions-Start). Wenn du das auch noch zu 100% ausschließen willst, wäre die Alternative, laufende mitmdump-Prozesse auf ihre log_id zu mappen — das ist deutlich fummeliger (Prozess-Env auf macOS auslesen) und fragiler. Meine Empfehlung ist die mtime-Schranke, weil sie 99% abdeckt und robust/einfach bleibt.

**Mein mentales Modell ist vollständig:** das Problem (stale Dual-Logs beendeter Alt-Versions-Sessions überleben die count-30-Rotation und werden nach Fix-Restart vom Pane gelesen), die betroffene Stelle (`_janitor_cleanup_jsonl_logs` in `claude_proxy_start.sh`, wo die count-30-Rotation lebt), und der Eingriff (additive Versions-Hash-Phase davor, mtime-geschützt). Kein Addon-Eingriff, keine externe Recherche nötig — alles eigener Bash-/Python-Code.

Zwei Entscheidungen für dich: (1) Hash nur über `proxy_addon.py` + `proxy/`, Start-Skript ausgenommen — einverstanden? (2) Live-Session-Schutz per mtime-Schranke (Empfehlung) oder doch die bulletproof-Prozess-Mapping-Variante?

You are a WORKER.

Your worktree: `/Users/brunowinter2000/Documents/ai/monitor-cc/.claude/worktrees/version-purge/`
This is your workspace — read, edit, test, and commit here. Do NOT touch files outside this path.

## Problem

The proxy writes dual-log files to `src/logs/dual_log/api_requests_<log_id>_<suffix>.jsonl`
(suffixes: original, forwarded, stripped, injected, errors, response). The monitor's proxy/worker
panes READ these files and render them. Today they are rotated ONLY count-30 (per opus/worker class)
by `_janitor_cleanup_jsonl_logs()` in `src/claude_proxy_start.sh`.

Problem: after a proxy CODE fix + restart, up to 30 OLD-version logs survive alongside the new ones.
The panes read them and show pre-fix renderings → false conclusion that the fix failed.

## Goal

Make the proxy-start janitor version-aware: when the proxy SOURCE changed since the last start,
purge the stale dual-logs of the old version.

## Design (LOCKED — implement exactly as specified, do NOT redesign)

1. **Proxy version = content hash** over exactly the live-copied proxy source: `src/proxy_addon.py`
   PLUS every file under `src/proxy/`. Hash must be stable / order-independent (sort the file list
   before hashing). Do NOT include `claude_proxy_start.sh` itself in the hash.

2. **Marker file:** `$LOG_DIR/dual_log/.proxy_version` — single line = the last-seen hash.

3. **At proxy start, additive, placed BEFORE the existing count-30 rotation** inside the janitor:
   - Compute current hash.
   - Read marker if present.
   - current hash == marker → NO version-purge (count-30 rotation runs as today).
   - current hash != marker, OR marker absent → version change:
     - Purge every file in `dual_log/` EXCEPT files modified in the last 60 minutes
       (i.e. delete files NOT touched in the last 60min — `find ... -mmin +60`-style).
     - Write current hash to the marker.
   - Absent marker = first version-aware start = treated as a change → this auto-cleans today's
     existing stale logs on the very first restart. No manual deletion required.

4. **Live-session protection — the 60min-mtime rule IS the whole mechanism.** A running session
   writes its logs continuously, so its files stay fresh (mtime < 60min) and survive the purge even
   on a version change. A session idle > 60min is treated as dead and purgeable. There is no separate
   liveness signal — just this one mtime rule.

## Investigation targets (READ FIRST)

- `src/claude_proxy_start.sh` — `_janitor_cleanup_jsonl_logs()` (count-30 rotation, where your phase
  hooks in), `_janitor_cleanup_live_copies()` (pgrep/orphan style reference), and the live-copy lines
  (`cp "$SCRIPT_DIR/proxy_addon.py"` + `cp -r "$SCRIPT_DIR/proxy"`) — these define exactly which files
  constitute the proxy version. Note `$LOG_DIR` and `$SCRIPT_DIR` are already defined in the script.
- `src/proxy_addon.py` — confirm it is the ~31-line entry shim (all logic lives in `src/proxy/`).

## Negative scope

- ONLY edit `src/claude_proxy_start.sh` (plus the dev smoke + `decisions/logging.md` IST below).
- Do NOT touch the `proxy/` package or `proxy_addon.py` — NO addon change.
- Do NOT add features beyond the version-purge.

## Deliverables

1. Version-purge phase in `_janitor_cleanup_jsonl_logs()` (or a dedicated helper called before it).
2. A bash smoke test under `dev/hook_smoke/` (analogous to the existing `test_log_janitor.py`)
   covering, in a TEMP dir (never touch real `src/logs/`): (a) version change purges stale (>60min)
   logs, (b) same version purges nothing, (c) fresh (<60min) logs survive a version-change purge,
   (d) absent marker triggers first-run cleanup. Make the purge logic sourceable/testable in isolation.
3. After implementing: update `decisions/logging.md` IST — document the new version-aware purge phase
   in the proxy-start janitor (extend the Zwei-Trigger / Janitor description; keep it dense, IST = what
   the code now does).

## FIRST: investigate + report

Read the files. Then describe: where exactly your phase hooks in, HOW you compute the stable hash in
bash (which tool, how you make it order-independent), HOW you implement the 60min-mtime guard, and HOW
the first-run (absent marker) path behaves. Then STOP and wait for Go before implementing.

Das `[RAG]`-Pane ist heute ausschließlich eine **Live-Indexing-Fortschrittsanzeige**. `RagController.tick()` liest alle 1,5s die Datei `~/.rag-locks/rag.lock` und zeigt `collection: done/total · elapsed` — aber nur, solange ein Indexing-Prozess diese Lock-Datei hält. Zwei konkrete Bugs erklären, was du siehst. Erstens das `0/0` während `update_docs`: ein normales `rag-cli index` meldet pro Dokument Fortschritt in den Lock, zeigt also z.B. „5/20" korrekt. `update_docs` (mein Session-End-Sync) meldet aber gar keinen Fortschritt — `sync.py` schreibt nie ins `progress`-Feld des Locks — also bleiben done/total bei 0/0, und weil `update_docs` keine einzelne Collection hat, fällt das Label auf „unknown" zurück. Daher „unknown: 0/0". Zweitens „Index wird gar nicht erkannt": bei einem schnellen Sync wird der Lock nur kurz gehalten, und der 1,5s-Poll verpasst ihn komplett.

Nebenbei aufgefallen: der Header-String im Code lautet „Sessions · [RAG] · Queue" — der „Beads"-Tab fehlt in genau diesem einen String (Drift gegen den dokumentierten 4-Tab-Ring). Kleinigkeit, die ziehe ich mit.

**Die Scope-Entscheidung, weil sie die Eingriffsfläche bestimmt:** Dein „x/x indexiert in dokumenten" kann zweierlei heißen.

Variante A — genauer Live-Fortschritt: bei *jedem* Lauf, auch `update_docs`, echtes „done/total Dokumente" anzeigen. Der Fix ist, `update_docs` dazu zu bringen, Fortschritt zu melden (rag-cli-Seite) plus das Menubar-Label. Im Leerlauf steht weiter „no indexing currently running".

Variante B — persistente Zählung: zusätzlich auch im Leerlauf „N Dokumente indexiert" pro Collection anzeigen, sodass das Pane immer etwas Sinnvolles zeigt. Das liest den tatsächlich indexierten Bestand (dieselbe Quelle, die das GPU-Pane schon über `rag-cli list_collections --json` nutzt).

Meine Empfehlung ist **beides**: genaues done/total während eines Laufs (killt das 0/0) und eine persistente Dokument-Zählung pro Collection im Leerlauf. Nur diese Version trifft dein „ich will x/x indexiert sehen", weil du das Pane meist anschaust, wenn gerade *nichts* läuft.

Zur Struktur: die Wurzel des 0/0 liegt in rag-cli (eigenes Projekt), die Anzeige in der Menubar von monitor-cc. Das berührt also zwei Repos, und die Menubar braucht am Ende einen py2app-Recompile. Bevor ich den Worker scope, lese ich noch den genauen rag-cli-Lock-Contract (lock.py/cli.py), damit done/total für `update_docs` exakt so gemeldet wird, wie die Menubar es erwartet.

📋 **PLAN — Step 3: Gap Analysis + Design + Worker Scope**

Der Fix für Variante A ist damit klar umrissen. In `sync.py`'s Dokument-Loop (`_sync_one_collection`, Zeile 150) wird `update_progress` eingezogen, genau wie es `index` in cli.py:342 macht. Dann zeigt die Menubar während `update_docs` echtes „done/total" statt 0/0.

Ein Detail zur Anzeige: das Label kommt in der Menubar aus `args.collection` des Locks (rag_controller.py:101). `update_docs` hat aber keine einzelne Collection in seinen Args — deshalb steht da „unknown". Damit nicht „unknown: 3/3" dasteht, würde ich das `progress`-Dict um ein optionales `collection`-Feld erweitern (rückwärtskompatibel), das `sync.py` pro Collection mitschreibt; die Menubar liest es als Label-Fallback. Ergebnis dann z.B. „monitor-cc-meta: 3/3 · 5s". Pro-Collection-Zählung ist hier sauberer als ein globaler Zähler, weil man sieht, welche Collection gerade läuft, und weil es exakt dem bestehenden `index`-Muster folgt.

Das berührt zwei Repos, also zwei Worker nacheinander. **Zuerst rag-cli** (definiert den Lock-Contract): `update_progress` um den optionalen `collection`-Parameter erweitern, in `sync.py` pro Dokument aufrufen, optional `index` mitziehen. **Danach monitor-cc** (liest den neuen Contract): der Menubar-Label-Fallback in `rag_controller.py` plus der py2app-Recompile am Schluss. So weiß der Menubar-Worker genau, welches Feld er liest.

## Problem

A consumer (the Monitor_CC menubar RAG panel) reads `~/.rag-locks/rag.lock` and renders the
`progress` dict as `{collection}: {done}/{total} · {elapsed}` while an indexing run holds the lock.

- `rag-cli index` reports progress correctly: its loop in `cli.py` calls
  `update_progress(done=i+1, total=len(to_index), current_document=document)` after each document
  (around cli.py:342).
- `rag-cli update_docs` does NOT: it dispatches to `sync_docs_workflow` (cli.py:350 →
  `src/rag/sync.py`), and the per-document loop in `_sync_one_collection` (sync.py ~line 150)
  never calls `update_progress`. So the lock's `progress` stays `{}` → the consumer shows `0/0`.
- Additionally `update_docs` has no single `args.collection`, so the consumer's label falls back
  to `unknown` → it shows `unknown: 0/0`.

## Goal

Make `update_docs` report real per-document progress to the lock, including the collection name,
so consumers see e.g. `monitor-cc-meta: 3/3` instead of `unknown: 0/0`.

## Design (LOCKED — implement as specified, do NOT redesign or refactor beyond this)

1. **`src/rag/lock.py` — extend `update_progress`** to accept an optional `collection`:
   `def update_progress(done: int, total: int, current_document: str, collection: str | None = None)`.
   Include `collection` in the written progress dict:
   `data["progress"] = {"done": done, "total": total, "current_document": current_document, "collection": collection}`.
   Backward-compatible: existing caller (cli.py:342, no `collection`) keeps working — `collection`
   defaults to `None`.

2. **`src/rag/sync.py` — wire `update_progress` into `_sync_one_collection`'s index loop.**
   Import `update_progress` from `.lock`. In the `for rel in to_index:` loop, after each
   `index_file(...)` + `upsert_hash(...)`, call:
   `update_progress(done=i + 1, total=len(to_index), current_document=rel, collection=collection)`
   (use `enumerate(to_index)` to get `i`). This is PER-COLLECTION — `total` = this collection's
   to_index count; the counter naturally resets when `sync_docs_workflow` moves to the next
   collection. That is intentional (the collection label changes with it).

3. **`cli.py` index path (~line 342) — optional consistency:** you MAY pass
   `collection=args.collection` to its `update_progress` call. Not required (index already has a
   collection label via args), but harmless and consistent. Your call — note your decision.

## Negative scope

- ONLY the progress-reporting wiring + the optional `collection` field. Nothing else.
- Do NOT touch the heartbeat mechanism, the lock acquire/release, or the indexing/embedding logic.
- Do NOT refactor `_sync_one_collection` into a global cross-collection counter.
- Do NOT add new CLI flags or features.

## Investigation targets (READ FIRST)

- `cli.py` ~lines 336–342 — the `index` loop's `update_progress` call (the pattern to mirror).

2. A focused test: if a test/dev convention exists (check `dev/` and how existing tests run),

## Problem

The RAG menubar panel (`src/menubar/rag_controller.py`, `_read_rag_status()`) reads
`~/.rag-locks/rag.lock` and renders `{collection}: {done}/{total} · {elapsed}` while an indexing
run holds the lock. The label `collection` is computed (rag_controller.py ~line 101) as:

```python
collection = args.get('collection') or Path(args.get('input', '')).name or 'unknown'
```

During a `rag-cli update_docs` run, the lock's `args` has NO `collection` key (update_docs takes a
project path, not a collection), so the label falls back to `'unknown'`.

## New contract (already landed on the rag-cli side, merged)

The rag-cli lock writer now includes a `collection` field inside the `progress` dict for
`update_docs` runs (per-collection name as it iterates). The lock's `progress` dict is now:

```json
"progress": {"done": 3, "total": 8, "current_document": "DOCS.md", "collection": "monitor-cc-meta"}
```

For a plain `rag-cli index` run, `args.collection` is set (so the existing label path still wins);
`progress.collection` is also present but redundant there.

## Goal

Make the menubar read `progress.collection` as a label fallback so update_docs shows e.g.
`monitor-cc-meta: 3/8 · 5s` instead of `unknown: 3/8 · 5s`.

## Design (LOCKED)

In `_read_rag_status()`: `progress` is currently read AFTER `collection` (lines ~101-102). Reorder
so `progress` is read first, then compute the label with `progress.get('collection')` inserted as a
fallback between `args.collection` and the `input`-name fallback:

```python
progress   = data.get('progress') or {}
collection = (args.get('collection')
              or progress.get('collection')
              or Path(args.get('input', '')).name
              or 'unknown')
```

Keep everything else byte-identical: the `kind` gate, the pid-alive check, the `done`/`total`
extraction, the `elapsed` format, the final f-string format. `progress.get('collection')` may be
`None` (e.g. old locks, or index locks where it's None) — the `or` chain handles that.

## Negative scope

- ONLY the label-fallback reorder in `_read_rag_status()`.
- Do NOT touch the kind-gate, pid logic, format string, the panel header string, the ring wiring,
  or any other method.
- Do NOT rebuild / recompile the py2app bundle — Opus does that post-merge from the merged tree.
- Do NOT add features.

## Investigation target (READ FIRST)

- `src/menubar/rag_controller.py` — `_read_rag_status()` (lines ~85-108), specifically the

   `{collection}: {done}/{total}` (real per-collection progress) instead of the old `unknown: 0/0`.

Discovered 2026-06-09 during #12 live-verify of the operation-transcript span-derivation. The
REQ-header `{n}strip {n}inj` badge (count of distinct strip/inject fns per request, via
`_strip_fns_lookup`/`_inject_fns_lookup`, built in `parser.py:accumulate_dual_log` from the
`_stripped`/`_injected` log `fn_map`) over-counts. Three distinct phantom sources, ALL verified
end-to-end on real log data — no open hypotheses.

Guiding principle (user, 2026-06-09): a strip/inject is badged ONLY when something **substantial**
happens — a real content injection like the bg-exit "background done" notification, or a real
content strip. Field overrides, structural placeholders, and spurious whitespace are NOT badge-worthy.

## Source 1 — Field-override attribution (REQ #33 class)

**Symptom:** REQ shows `1inj` (no strip); the only change is a top-level field override
(model / effort / thinking / max_tokens). No content injected — phantom.

**Root cause:** `logging.py:358` (`s_fn_map`) and `:364` (`i_fn_map`) write the field-change fn
(`_FIELD_STRIP_FN` / `_FIELD_INJECT_FN`, default `_inject_model_override`) into `fn_map` with loc_key
`field.{key}`. The badge counts `set(fn_map.values())`, so field overrides count as content
strip/inject. The proxy overrides these fields on EVERY request → nearly every request would badge an
inject → ruins the visualization. The `fields:` drill-down already shows them (orig→fwd diff).
Why "inj without strip" at #33: the field fn_map is written only when the per-field hash changes vs
the previous request (`logging.py:356/362`); at #33 the FORWARDED override values changed (→ inject
hash changed → inj fn) while CC's ORIGINAL values were stable (→ strip hash unchanged → no strip fn).

**Fix:** write-side — drop the field-change attribution from `s_fn_map`/`i_fn_map` (logging.py
358/364). Fields belong in the `fields:` drill-down, never the content badge. Revert the read-side
`field.*` filter (`parser.py:158`) — it was a fallback masking this write-side over-attribution;
`fn_map`'s ONLY consumer is the badge, so the field entries are dead data, removed at source.

## Source 2 — Partial-strip net-new newline (REQ #115 class; msg.36.0, msg.302.1)

**Symptom:** `1strip 1inj` where the strip is real + visible but the "inj" is an invisible/meaningless
fragment `</system-reminder>\n`.

**Root cause:** `strip_sr.py:147` (partial mode) ALWAYS re-appends `\n` to the closing tag. The match
regex `_STANDALONE_SR_RE` ends with `\n?` (optionally consumes a trailing newline — needed for the
full-block-removal mode). Partial mode RESTORES the consumed `\n` — but unconditionally. When the
original had NO trailing `\n`, the regex consumed none yet the append adds one → a NET-NEW `\n` in the
forwarded payload. `_extract_block_op` then sees a tail diff (`</system-reminder>` vs
`</system-reminder>\n`) → op `injected="</system-reminder>\n"` → `("injected", …)` span →
`has_i=True` → fn_map (`unknown`) → phantom badge. This is also a real proxy-output bug: a spurious
newline forwarded to the API. Verified end-to-end (flow 7ab56ecd; `compose_block` Inv1/Inv2 PASS — the
composition is correct, the bad op originates upstream in the strip).

**Fix:** write-side `strip_sr.py:147` — restore the `\n` only if the matched original had one
(`trailing_nl = '\n' if full.endswith('\n') else ''`). Removes the spurious newline AND the phantom.
NOT "remove the append entirely" — that would drop the legitimate separator when the original HAD a
`\n`, gluing the following content onto the tag. CI invariant `test_composition_invariant.py` SAFE
(structural composition unchanged, cleaner op).

## Source 3 — Empty-block "." placeholder (msg.0.0 / msg.0.1, REQ#1 only)

**Symptom:** `1inj` where the injected text is just ".".

**Root cause:** `strip_sr.py` `... or '.'` (lines 73/83/94) replaces a fully-stripped block's empty
content with "." (intentional — Anthropic rejects empty content blocks). The "." then surfaces as an
injected span → `has_i=True` → fn_map (`unknown`) → phantom badge.

**Fix:** write-side `logging.py` (the `i_fn_map` assignment, ~330-341) — skip the fn_map attribution
when the injected text is just ".". The `or '.'` itself STAYS (intentional proxy correctness — it is
primarily a strip, the "." only exists to let the message pass the API; it is not an injection).

## Fix Plan (ordered, one stage)

1. `parser.py:158` — revert read-side `field.*` filter back to `set(fn_map.values())`.
2. `logging.py:358` + `:364` — drop field-change attribution from `s_fn_map` / `i_fn_map`.
3. `strip_sr.py:147` — preserve the original trailing-`\n` state.
4. `logging.py` ~330-341 — skip the `i_fn_map` attribution when the injected text is just ".".
5. Verify on real `_injected`/`_stripped` logs: field-only reqs → no badge; the `</system-reminder>\n`
   and `.` phantoms gone; bg-exit + real content injects still badge. Update IST: `src/proxy/DOCS.md`,
   `src/proxy_display/DOCS.md`, `decisions/logging.md`.

Jetzt der Successor-Prompt mit den vier Fixes, der die OldThemes-Datei als Spec liest.", "type": "text"}, {"preview": {"content": "# Worker Task: Fix the three strip/inject badge false-positive sources

You are a WORKER.

Your worktree: `/Users/brunowinter2000/Documents/ai/monitor-cc/.claude/worktrees/badge-fix/`
This is your workspace — read, edit, test, and commit here. Do NOT touch files outside this path.

## Context — read this FIRST

The full investigation + ordered fix plan is in
`decisions/OldThemes/proxy_tool_stripping/17_badge_false_positives.md`. Read it completely. It
documents three VERIFIED root causes for phantom strip/inject badge counts in the proxy pane, and
the exact fix for each. Below is the implementation summary; the OldThemes doc is the authority.

Guiding principle: a strip/inject is badged ONLY when something **substantial** happens (a real
content injection like the bg-exit "background done" notification, or a real content strip). Field
overrides, structural "." placeholders, and spurious whitespace are NOT badge-worthy.

## The four edits (one coherent stage)

1. **`src/proxy_display/parser.py:158`** — REVERT the read-side `field.*` filter back to the original
   `acc.setdefault('_fns_by_flow_id', {})[fid] = set((entry.get('fn_map') or {}).values())`.
   (It was a fallback; the real fix is write-side below, so this filter is no longer needed.)

2. **`src/proxy/logging.py:358` and `:364`** — drop the field-change attribution. These lines write
   `s_fn_map[lk]` / `i_fn_map[lk]` for `field.{key}` loc_keys. Remove those two assignments so field
   overrides never enter `fn_map` (they are already shown in the `fields:` drill-down; the proxy
   overrides model/effort/thinking on every request, so attributing them ruins the badge). Keep the
   `s_fields[key]` / `i_fields[key]` writes (the fields_delta itself must stay) — only the fn_map
   assignment for fields is removed.

3. **`src/proxy/strip_sr.py:147`** — preserve the original trailing-`\n` state instead of always
   appending `\n`. The partial-strip return currently does
   `return '<system-reminder>' + cleaned + '</system-reminder>\n'`. Change to add `\n` only if the
   matched original text had one — e.g. `trailing_nl = '\n' if full.endswith('\n') else ''` then
   `... + '</system-reminder>' + trailing_nl`. (`full` = the regex-matched text; confirm the actual
   variable name in scope.) This removes the spurious newline AND the phantom inject. Do NOT remove
   the append entirely — that drops the legitimate separator when the original HAD a `\n`.

4. **`src/proxy/logging.py` (~330-341, the message `i_fn_map` block)** — when the injected text for a
   block is just "." (the empty-block placeholder), SKIP the `i_fn_map[lk]` assignment so it does not
   badge. Keep the `or '.'` placeholder itself (it is intentional — Anthropic rejects empty blocks).
   The block is primarily a strip; the "." is not an injection.

## Negative scope

- Only the four edits above. Do NOT touch `compose_block`, `apply_edit_to_spans`, `diff_engine.py`,
  the renderers, or the `or '.'` placeholder logic in `strip_sr.py`.
- Do NOT change the `_STANDALONE_SR_RE` regex (the `\n?` is needed for the full-block-removal mode).

## CI invariant

`dev/proxy_dual_log/test_composition_invariant.py` MUST stay green. Run it after your changes. The
OldThemes doc states the strip_sr fix is structurally safe for it — confirm by running.

## FIRST: investigate + report

Read the OldThemes doc + the four target sites. Describe each exact edit (paste the before/after for
strip_sr.py:147 and the logging.py field/`.` changes), confirm the `s_fields`/`i_fields` writes are
preserved, and confirm `test_composition_invariant.py` still passes conceptually. Then STOP and wait
for Go before implementing.

## Deliverables

1. The four edits.
2. Verification on REAL logs (`src/logs/dual_log/api_requests_*_injected.jsonl` /
   `*_stripped.jsonl`): show that (a) field-only entries no longer contribute to the badge set,
   (b) the `</system-reminder>\n` and `.` injected phantoms no longer produce a fn_map entry,
   (c) real injects (bg-exit "background done") and real content strips STILL badge. Also run
   `test_composition_invariant.py` → must pass.
3. IST updates: `src/proxy/DOCS.md` (strip_sr.py + logging.py notes), `src/proxy_display/DOCS.md`
   (revert the field.* badge note added by the prior task), `decisions/logging.md` (the fn_map
   attribution rules: fields excluded, `.`-injection excluded).

🛠️ **IMPROVE+CLOSE**", "type": "text"}, {"preview": {"content": "# Proxy Version-Aware Dual-Log Purge

Issue #13 (closed). 2026-06-09. The proxy-start janitor (`claude_proxy_start.sh`) now purges stale
dual-logs of a PREVIOUS proxy version on a version change, so a fix-restart no longer leaves
old-version renderings in the proxy pane (which caused false "fix failed" conclusions).

## Problem

Dual-logs (`src/logs/dual_log/api_requests_<id>_<suffix>.jsonl`) drive the proxy/worker panes. They
were rotated ONLY count-30 (per opus/worker class) by `_janitor_cleanup_jsonl_logs`. After a proxy
CODE fix + restart, up to 30 old-version logs survived alongside the new ones → the pane read them →
pre-fix renderings → false conclusion the fix failed. (Not the time-based `cleanup_old_jsonl` path —
that only handles hook_firing / api_errors / polling_state.)

## Design (implemented)

Additive Phase 0 in `_janitor_cleanup_jsonl_logs`, before the count-30 rotation:
1. Content-hash the live-copied proxy source: `proxy_addon.py` + `proxy/**/*.py` + `proxy/**/*.json`
   (sort-stable). Marker `dual_log/.proxy_version` = last-seen hash.
2. On hash != marker (or marker absent = first run): delete dual-logs not modified in the last 60min
   (`find -mmin +60`); write the new hash.

## Decisions

**Hash scope = `proxy_addon.py` + `proxy/`** (exactly the live-copied set, what actually runs). Start
script EXCLUDED (orchestration, not request-processing; tweaking the janitor itself shouldn't purge).
`.json` schemas INCLUDED (`proxy/schemas/github-research/*.json` = injected tool definitions =
behavioral; a schema change alters the forwarded payload). `__pycache__/*.pyc`, `DOCS.md`, `.DS_Store`
EXCLUDED (recompile noise / docs — would trigger false version changes).

**Live-session protection = the 60min-mtime rule IS the mechanism, not a fallback** (user, 2026-06-09:
"räume alle Logs ab außer es gab einen Eintrag < 60min"). A running session writes its logs
continuously → fresh mtime → survives the purge even on a version change; a session idle > 60min is
treated as dead. Rejected the deterministic alternative (pgrep + `ps`-env mapping of running
mitmdumps): fragile macOS `ps`-parsing, and any crash-safe liveness signal (sentinel/marker) needs a
time-fallback anyway because a crashed process leaves no clean signal — so the time rule is the
simplest crash-safe option, not a compromise.

**No addon change** — pure `claude_proxy_start.sh`. **First-run auto-cleanup** — an absent marker is
treated as a change, so the very first version-aware restart purges the pre-existing stale logs; no
manual deletion.

## Status

Implemented in `claude_proxy_start.sh` (`_compute_proxy_hash` + `_janitor_version_purge_jsonl_logs`).
Smoke: `dev/hook_smoke/test_version_purge.sh` (4 cases, 8/8). IST: `decisions/logging.md`
(Trigger 1 / Phase 0). LIVE-VERIFY pending — requires a real proxy code change + restart.

2026-06-09. The menubar RAG pane (`RagController`, reads `~/.rag-locks/rag.lock`) is a LIVE
indexing-progress indicator. During `rag-cli update_docs` it showed `unknown: 0/0` — `sync.py` never
called `lock.update_progress`, and update_docs has no single `args.collection`.

## Decision

**Variante A chosen** — accurate live done/total during any run (incl. update_docs). **Variante B
rejected** — a persistent "N docs indexed per collection" count in the idle state. User: "wir
überladen das nicht." The pane stays a live indicator; when nothing runs it shows "no indexing
currently running".

## Mechanism (implemented)

- rag-cli `lock.update_progress(done, total, current_document, collection=None)` — optional
  `collection` added to the progress dict (`src/rag/lock.py`).
- `sync.py:_sync_one_collection` calls it per document (per-collection counter; resets on the next
  collection — intentional, the label shows which collection runs). cli.py `index` also passes
  `collection=args.collection`.
- Menubar `_read_rag_status`: label = `args.collection or progress.collection or Path(args.input).name
  or 'unknown'`. Result: `monitor-cc-docs: 3/8 · 5s` instead of `unknown: 0/0`.

## Status

Implemented + LIVE-VERIFIED — the lock carried real `done/total/current_document/collection` during a
real update_docs run (1→4 of 4, collection `monitor-cc-docs`); menubar rendering sample-tested. IST:
`decisions/menubar_rag_tab.md`, rag-cli `decisions/infra02_lock_and_status.md`.

## #5 — der gestaffelte src/-Port (OldTheme 16)

Das ist die Auflösung von zwei Live-Bugs aus dem #5-Bundle (beide am 06-08 aufgetaucht): das **Green-Overlay-False-Injection** (unveränderter Content wird grün als "injiziert" gerendert) und der **bg-exit-Double-Inject** (`_WAKEUP_TEXT` wird auf einem einzigen Block doppelt grün gezeichnet — dein Screenshot "doppelt der inj gerendert").

Die Diagnose hat beide Bugs sauber in **Layer 2** verortet: Was der Proxy wirklich *sendet* (Layer 1) ist korrekt, der Renderer (Layer 3) zeigt treu an — falsch sind die abgeleiteten `_stripped`/`_injected`-Span-Logs dazwischen. Die Ursache ist, dass die Passes nur Text-*Chunks* pro Message-Index speichern, ohne Position und Reihenfolge. Der Span-Builder sucht die Chunk-Positionen dann zur Build-Zeit per `fwd_text.find(chunk)` neu — und findet denselben Chunk zweimal (Double-Inject) oder fällt auf einen Word-Level-Diff zurück, der einen geteilten JSON-Präfix fälschlich als injiziert taggt (Phantom). `_dedup_wakeup_blocks` und der Diff-Fallback sind Patches auf Patches — fix einen, der nächste poppt auf.

Das neue Modell ist ein **Operation-Transcript**: jede Modifikation wird im Moment, in dem der Pass sie macht, als positions-verankerte Op `(block_idx, offset, removed, injected)` aufgezeichnet und alle Ops werden deterministisch zu einer Span-Liste komponiert — kein Re-Search, kein Dedup, kein Fallback. Die Vollständigkeit ist eine Code-Eigenschaft (endlich viele Mutations-Sites), abgesichert durch die Rekonstruktions-Invariante `original + ops == forwarded` als CI-Test statt als Runtime-Netz.

Entscheidend: **das ist schon per dev/-Probe an echten Daten bewiesen** — 7219/7219 Blocks byte-exact über 484 modifizierte Requests / 5 Stems, inklusive der Money-Shot-Message mit TN+BG-Double, die jetzt zu genau einer Injektion komponiert. Offen ist nur noch der **src/-Port**, und der ist bewusst gestaffelt geplant: pass-by-pass präzises Recording → Composition-Builder nach src/ → Invariante als CI-Test → Fallbacks (`_diff_text` + `_dedup`) entfernen, je Stage mit eigenem Sign-off. Die Dateien sind `src/proxy/rules.py`, `diff_engine.py`, `logging.py`. Noch ungeklärt im Port: sidecar/idle_recap fehlen im Corpus (Theorie-Modell steht), und das TN-Transform-Recording ist der kniffligste Teil, weil es den XML-Wrapper strippt aber den Innentext behält — kein sauberes Chunk-Removal. Das ist mit "zwei Queries und du bist drin" gemeint: `proxy_tool_stripping/16_operation_transcript_redesign.md` plus der Probe-Report tragen den kompletten Plan.

## #10 — die Directives-Read-Seite

Hier ist der Write-Side-Teil schon gelandet: `context_management` und `diagnostics` (Request-Body-Direktiven) werden seit 06-08 in `_build_forwarded_delta` (logging.py) ins `_forwarded`-Log mitgeschrieben. Was fehlt, ist die **Read-Seite** — das Rendering dieser Direktiven im REQUEST-Teil der Proxy-Pane. Das ist doppelt geblockt: der Rendering-Code existiert noch nicht, *und* live-verifizieren geht erst nach einem Proxy-Restart mit echtem Traffic, weil bis dahin keine `_forwarded`-Einträge die Direktiven tatsächlich tragen.

## sidecar / idle_recap — warum "fehlen im Corpus"

Das sind zwei Sonderfälle von Requests, die der Proxy nicht im normalen Multi-Pass-Loop behandelt, sondern per **Short-Circuit** ganz am Anfang abfängt. Beide erkennt er an einer Struktur-Signatur:

**sidecar** (`_detect_sidecar`) ist ein Request mit genau einer User-Message, plain-string-Content und praktisch leerem System-Block (≤10 Zeichen), kein Haiku — das sind die kleinen Neben-Calls, die CC abseits der Hauptkonversation macht. **idle_recap** (`_detect_idle_recap`) ist die letzte User-Message, deren Content mit `"The user stepped away and is coming back."` beginnt — CCs Idle-Wiederaufnahme-Injektion.

In beiden Fällen ersetzt der Proxy den **kompletten** Content durch einen Marker — `[SIDECAR_STRIPPED_<N>_BYTES]` bzw. `[IDLE_RECAP_STRIPPED_<N>_BYTES]`. Das ist die simpelste denkbare Modifikation: alles raus, ein Marker rein. Im Op-Transcript-Modell ist das `Op(block_idx=0, offset=0, removed=ganzer_original, injected=marker)` — eine triviale Voll-Ersetzung, die garantiert sauber komponiert, weil es nur einen einzigen Op über den ganzen Block gibt, ohne Überlappung, ohne Reihenfolge-Problem.

Der Haken ist nicht das Modell, sondern die **Beweislage**. Der dev/-Probe, der die 7219/7219 byte-exact gezeigt hat, lief über einen Corpus aus 5 echten Log-Stems — und in genau diesen 5 Stems kam kein einziger sidecar- oder idle_recap-Request vor. Das heißt: für diese zwei Pfade ist die Op-Form *theoretisch* klar (Voll-Ersetzung, trivial), aber sie wurde nie gegen einen echten erfassten Eintrag durch die Rekonstruktions-Invariante (`original + ops == forwarded`) gejagt. Und genau das ist die Disziplin, auf der das ganze Redesign steht: ein Code-Pfad, der mutiert, ohne dass seine Aufzeichnung empirisch geprüft ist, ist exakt die Sorte Lücke, die die Invariante fangen soll. Deshalb steht im Plan, dass der Port "mit echten Daten schließen" muss — also einen realen sidecar+idle_recap einfangen und die Invariante drüberlaufen lassen, statt darauf zu vertrauen, dass der triviale Fall schon stimmt.

## TN-Transform — warum "kein sauberes Chunk-Removal"

Hier liegt der eigentliche Schwierigkeitsgrad. Schau dir die anderen First-Pass-Branches an — plan-mode, deferred-tools, task-tools-nag: die sind alle **saubere Strips**. Sie finden einen literalen, zusammenhängenden Chunk (`_find_system_reminder_blocks(...)`), der komplett verschwindet. Die Op ist "dieser Chunk → nichts", also `(offset, removed_chunk, '')`. Positions-verankert aufzuzeichnen ist da unkompliziert: das, was rausfällt, ist genau das, was vorher als Chunk dastand.

Die Task-Notification-Branch ist strukturell anders. `_strip_task_notification_tags` matcht zwar den ganzen `<task-notification>…</task-notification>`-Block, aber die Ersetzungsfunktion `_extract` **löscht ihn nicht** — sie sucht das `<summary>…</summary>` darin und gibt den **Innentext** zurück (`summary.strip() + '\n'`). Der XML-Wrapper fällt weg, der Summary-Text bleibt erhalten. Das ist kein Removal, das ist ein **Transform**: ein Stück Text in der Mitte wird umgeschrieben, ein Teil verschwindet (die Tags), ein Teil überlebt (der Summary-Inhalt) — und der überlebende Teil ist eine *Teilzeichenkette des entfernten Bereichs*. Genau das macht die chunk-basierte Aufzeichnung kaputt: Wo endet "entfernt" und wo beginnt "behalten", wenn das Behaltene mitten im Entfernten steckt? Die Op lässt sich nicht als sauberes `(removed, '')` schreiben, sondern nur als `(prefix_len, changed_region, new_region)` — wobei `changed_region` der ganze originale XML-Block und `new_region` der extrahierte Summary-Text ist.

Und es kommt zusammen: dieselbe TN-Branch hängt danach noch `_WAKEUP_TEXT` an (`_append_wakeup_text_to_content`) und strippt eventuell zusätzlich den "task tools haven't been used"-Nag. Eine einzige TN-Message kann also bis zu drei komponierte Ops tragen — den Wrapper-Transform, den Nag-Strip und die Wakeup-Injektion. Die Wakeup-Injektion auf einem *failed* bg-Task ist genau die Stelle, an der der Double-Inject-Bug saß. Damit ist TN der einzige Message-Typ, der gleichzeitig (a) einen Transform-statt-Strip, (b) mehrere Ops auf einer Message und (c) eine Injektion ausübt — und deshalb der kniffligste Teil im Port. Der alte Ansatz hat den entfernten Text als Chunk gespeichert und die Position zur Build-Zeit per `find()` neu gesucht, was hier zwingend scheitert, weil entfernter Wrapper und behaltener Innentext sich im Original überlappen. Im neuen Modell muss `_strip_task_notification_tags` (bzw. sein Aufrufer) den präzisen Offset plus beide Regionen direkt im Moment des Strippens emittieren — eine deutlich invasivere Änderung als bei den sauberen Strip-Passes.

Kurz: sidecar/idle_recap sind trivial-aber-unbewiesen (Voll-Ersetzung, fehlte im Test-Corpus), TN ist nachgewiesen-aber-komplex (Transform mit überlappendem Keep-Bereich plus Mehrfach-Op plus Injektion). ## Was Restart braucht, was direkt geht

Die entscheidende Trennlinie: **alles, was die Korrektheit des Kompositions-Modells gegen bereits erfasste Logs prüft, ist direkt** — der Probe und der CI-Invariant-Test lesen die existierenden `_original`/`_forwarded`-Dateien von Platte, kein Proxy-Restart, kein neuer Traffic. Das gilt für TN (schon bewiesen) und für sidecar/idle_recap (jetzt machbar). Der gesamte Port lässt sich offline bauen und beweisen.

**Nur eine einzige Sache braucht einen Proxy-Restart**: der finale Live-Verify. Sobald das neue Operation-Transcript-Recording in `src/proxy/` (rules.py, diff_engine.py, logging.py) liegt, muss der Proxy neu starten, damit *der laufende Proxy* den neuen Code auf Live-Traffic ausführt und neue `_stripped`/`_injected`-Logs im neuen Format schreibt. Erst dann rendert die Monitor-Pane sie und du bestätigst an echten Screenshots, dass Green-False-Injection und bg-exit-Double-Inject weg sind. Die Render-Seite (Layer 3) ändert sich nicht — sie war nie der Bug —, also ist es ausschließlich ein Proxy-Restart, kein Monitor-Thema.

Pointiert: der Port wird **komplett offline gegen erfasste Logs entwickelt und bewiesen**, und der Restart ist ein einziges Bestätigungs-Gate am Ende, das nur zeigt "der In-Proxy-Code produziert dasselbe, was der Offline-Probe schon byte-exact bewiesen hat".

## Wie es aufeinander aufbaut

Die Abhängigkeitskette, in Reihenfolge:

**Stufe 0 — Corpus-Lücke schließen (dev/, direkt, kein Restart).** Den `composition_probe` über den vollen Stem-Satz inklusive der sidecar/idle_recap-tragenden Stems laufen lassen. Das de-riskt den ganzen Port, weil das Modell danach für *alle* Op-Shapes bewiesen ist, und erledigt die Trust-oder-Rip-Frage empirisch. Reine dev/-Arbeit, kein src/-Touch. Das ist der logische erste Schritt.

**Stufe 1 — Pass-by-pass präzises Recording in src/.** Jeder Pass in `rules.py` emittiert positions-verankerte Ops statt Chunk-Listen. TN ist der harte Fall (Transform-Recording mit überlappendem Keep-Bereich). Direkt unit-testbar — Payload rein, Ops raus, Invariante assert. Kein Restart für die Unit-Tests.

**Stufe 2 — Composition-Builder nach src/.** Den im Probe bewiesenen Algorithmus in `diff_engine.py` ziehen, `build_message_spans`/`_diff_text` ersetzen; die `_stripped`/`_injected`-Deltas in `logging.py` leiten sich jetzt aus komponierten Ops ab. Konsumiert die Ops aus Stufe 1 — **baut also zwingend auf Stufe 1 auf**. Direkt gegen erfasste Logs testbar.

**Stufe 3 — Invariante als CI-Test.** `original + ops == forwarded` über den Corpus als Regressionstest festschreiben. Braucht Stufe 2 (die Komposition muss existieren). Direkt.

**Stufe 4 — Fallbacks entfernen.** `_diff_text`-Fallback + `_dedup_wakeup_blocks`-Span-Patch löschen. Erst wenn der CI-Invariant aus Stufe 3 die Korrektheit absichert — **baut auf Stufe 3 auf**. Direkt.

**Stufe 5 — Live-Verify (der einzige Restart).** Proxy-Restart, neuer Code auf Live-Traffic, neue Logs, Render, deine Bestätigung an echten Screenshots.

Die Reihenfolge ist nicht beliebig: 0 entkoppelt und de-riskt vorab; 1→2→3→4 ist eine harte Kette (Recording vor Komposition vor Invariant vor Fallback-Entfernung); 5 bestätigt am Ende, dass das offline Bewiesene live identisch läuft. Genau diese Staffelung — jede Stufe ein eigener committbarer, verifizierbarer Schnitt mit eigenem Sign-off — ist das, was im OldTheme als "staged port per Sequential Sub-Stage Decomposition" steht, und verhindert das Überhitzen, in das ein monolithisches "bau den ganzen Port" sonst läuft.

## Context

The repo has a dev/ validation probe at `dev/proxy_dual_log/composition_probe.py`. It proves that the proxy's per-pass content modifications, recorded as position-anchored operations `(offset, removed, injected)` and composed via span-list accumulation, reconstruct both the original (C0) and the forwarded (Cfwd) content byte-exact. It runs over a corpus of captured dual-logs and emits a markdown report under `dev/proxy_dual_log/01_reports/`.

The probe currently validates the multi-pass modification chain (first_pass, cumulative_sr, bg_exit, dedup_wakeup, etc.) — 7219/7219 blocks byte-exact. But it does NOT cover two short-circuit modification paths: **sidecar** and **idle_recap**. The report's "Sidecar / Idle-Recap Coverage" section says `UNVERIFIED — not present in 5-stem corpus`.

That statement is misleading. The data IS in the corpus — the probe just never processes it. Diagnostic findings (verified by Opus):

- `run_passes_and_collect_ops` only runs the multi-pass functions; the production short-circuits are absent from its sequence. A sidecar/idle_recap payload therefore produces zero ops, and `run_corpus` skips it via `if not ops: continue`.
- Across the 5 corpus stems, 2 sidecar + 1 idle_recap entries are already present but silently skipped.
- The on-disk stem `api_requests_opus_monitor_cc_1780951598` is NOT in the probe's `LOG_STEMS` and carries 5 more sidecar + 5 more idle_recap entries.

The production short-circuits live in `src/proxy/`:
- `_detect_sidecar` / `_detect_idle_recap` (in `src/proxy/payload_helpers.py`) — the structural detectors.
- `_check_sidecar` / `_check_idle_recap` (in `src/proxy/rules.py`) — short-circuit handlers. They fire BEFORE the multi-pass chain and bypass it entirely. Each replaces the entire message content with a marker string and returns a full 7-tuple that ALREADY carries the explicit original + injected marker (unlike multi-pass passes, which only record chunk lists). Their operation shape is the trivial full-replacement: `Op(msg_idx, offset=0, removed=full_original_content, injected=marker_string)`.

## Task

Extend the probe so it covers the sidecar/idle_recap short-circuit path, then re-run it over a corpus that includes those entries.

1. Status: **REMOVED + merged on dev** (commit `6da5410`, merge `2522af9`). The two short-circuit strips `_check_sidecar` / `_check_idle_recap` and their detectors are gone. This file is the demotion target for the old IST history (formerly in `decisions/pipe05_proxy_cache.md`) + the removal rationale.

## What existed (demoted from pipe05 IST)

- `stripped_sidecar_content` (added commit `54d743e`, 2026-04-23): `_check_sidecar` short-circuited early in `apply_modification_rules` (`src/proxy/rules.py`). Detected CC-internal sidecar requests via `_detect_sidecar` (`src/proxy/payload_helpers.py`): single-msg, plain-string content, empty system (≤10 chars), non-haiku. Replaced `messages[0].content` with marker `[SIDECAR_STRIPPED_<n>_BYTES]` before forwarding. Original rationale: a single sidecar injection once cost ~24k CC tokens (evidence: session 1776956156 REQ#80.1, 49,586c content). Short-circuit placement avoided a spurious `stripped_all_sr_msg0` on the marker.
- `stripped_idle_recap`: `_check_idle_recap` short-circuited the CC idle-recap injection via `_detect_idle_recap`: last-msg user, plain-string content starting with `"The user stepped away and is coming back."`. Replaced content with `[IDLE_RECAP_STRIPPED_<n>_BYTES]`.

Both bypassed the multi-pass span-derivation entirely (short-circuit BEFORE the pass chain). The op shape was the trivial full-replacement `Op(idx, 0, full_original, marker)`; the 7-tuple already carried `stripped_msg_originals` + `injected_msg_added` explicitly.

## Why removed — the use case is dead

CC 1.49 changed the payload structure. The detectors require **plain-string** content; current single-message non-haiku requests carry **list** content → `_detect_sidecar` never matches. `_detect_idle_recap` requires a last-msg plain-string starting with the idle marker → not present in current traffic. The strip targets a payload form CC no longer produces.

Decision: **remove**, not verify-synthetic, not blind-trust. Policy = observation over mutation (proxy pane = single source of truth). Stop mutating; rely on full-forward visibility in the proxy pane as the safety net. If a new sidecar-like structure ever appears, it becomes visible in the pane (full content forwarded → logged → rendered) and we react then. Removing a content-replacing strip IMPROVES visibility — real content flows to `_forwarded` instead of a marker.

## Investigation (how "absent from corpus" was confirmed real)

The `composition_probe` baseline (2026-06-08) reported `sidecar/idle_recap UNVERIFIED — absent from 5-stem corpus`. Two reframings settled it:

| Step | Method | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Opus initial (WRONG) | naive `grep stripped_sidecar_content` over dual_log | 127 sidecar / 66 idle_recap "matches" → concluded "data exists, just not in probe corpus" |
| Worker cross-check | production detectors `_detect_sidecar`/`_detect_idle_recap` over all 9 stems + `_stripped.jsonl` mod-records | **0 / 0** — corpus never captured a real sidecar/idle_recap |
| Opus verify | detector on stem `1780951598` + forwarded `modifications` field + match-context | detector 0/0; modifications field 0; raw matches are source-code-in-payload — first hit literally `src/proxy/rules.py:208: ["stripped_sidecar_content"]` |

The naive grep was fooled by monitor-cc dev sessions sending the proxy's OWN source (`rules.py`/`strip_vocab.py`, which contain the literal strings) as message payloads. The original "absent from corpus" was literally accurate. Cross-model divergence (worker contradicted Opus with stronger methodology) → Opus re-verified directly → worker correct.

## Safety condition (user) — verified

Full forwarded content must stay visible in the proxy pane. Worker-traced in `src/proxy/addon.py`: full original recorded before modification; with no short-circuit the payload returns unchanged; `_build_stripped_injected_deltas` finds zero changes (empty deltas); the proxy pane renders via `_build_forwarded_delta` as a standalone `'S'` entry (`_is_standalone_entry`, sys=0/tools=0) with full content. No drop path.

## Removal surface

- `src/proxy/rules.py` — `_check_sidecar` / `_check_idle_recap` defs + call-sites + imports (orchestrator now falls straight into the multi-pass chain)
- `src/proxy/payload_helpers.py` — `_detect_sidecar` / `_detect_idle_recap`
- `src/proxy/strip_vocab.py` — `SC` / `IR` RULES entries + `_SR_STRIP_RULES` exclusion (`('TN','SC','IR','PP')` → `('TN','PP')`, comment fixed to PP)
- `src/proxy/logging.py` — `SC` / `IR` in `_MSG_CODE_TO_FN`
- `decisions/pipe05_proxy_cache.md` IST — `stripped_sidecar_content` entry removed
- `src/proxy/DOCS.md` — `rules.py` module entry (LOC 584→532, inject-points 4→3, Purpose + helper list)
- `dev/proxy_dual_log/` — `green_overlay_probe.py`, `composition_probe.py` (sidecar coverage block), `attribution_coverage.py`, `verify_delta.py`, `DOCS.md`

Ich schreibe das OldThemes (chat-derived, also ich direkt), dann die Issue in `GlobalRules`, dann commit+push.", "type": "text"}, {"preview": {"content": "# Worker Orchestration — Cross-Worker Persistence Model (2026-06-08)

Status: DISCUSSED. Current model AFFIRMED (rules as-is, no change). Residual tension tracked as a GlobalRules issue.

## Origin

Surfaced during the monitor-cc proxy operation-transcript port (staged Stage 1). A worker investigated Stage 1 fully (cross-model check), then died mid-implementation of Sub-Stage 1A at ~24% remaining context — uncommitted, in-flight work at the context wall. Recovery: Opus manually committed the WIP (with a SUCCESSOR-HANDOFF), spawned a fresh successor that merged the WIP branch and finished 1A. Raised the question: how do implementation workers benefit from the investigation across the worker's 200k context limit?

## The tension

- The cross-model check REQUIRES the worker to investigate the full picture independently — that independent second model IS the verification value.
- Full investigation is context-expensive (the worker reads everything).
- A large implementation task then exhausts the SAME worker's context mid-stage → death uncommitted.
- So full investigation (needed for cross-model) competes with implementation budget inside one 200k worker.

## Rejected alternative (Opus proposal, 2026-06-08)

"Investigation worker writes its plan as a persistent report on disk; each implementation sub-stage worker reads the report (cheap file-read) instead of relying on living context; Opus just points at the report."

Rejected by user. Reasons:
- **Opus has ~1M context, not 200k. Opus IS the persistence layer — that is precisely the orchestrator's role, enabled by the larger context window.**
- Disk-report-per-worker adds file-write overhead, double-reading (write then read), and process friction for no gain over Opus simply holding the report.
- It distributes what should be centralized: the investigation lives in ONE place — Opus's context.

## Affirmed model (rules as-is)

1. Worker investigates the FULL picture — no splitting of the investigation.
2. Worker reports to Opus. Opus holds the report in its ~1M context (the cross-worker shared memory).
3. Opus cuts the implementation into small sub-steps (Sequential Sub-Stage Decomposition).
4. Each sub-step is sized to be a committable unit a worker finishes in a bounded turn; we hope each worker closes one sub-step.
5. On worker death mid-stage, Opus spawns a precise successor from its mental-model + the held report + the dying worker's WIP commit/handoff. No re-investigation, no file archaeology.

Opus's 1M context is the structural enabler that removes any need for a disk-based cross-worker plan-report. The existing rules (`opus/workers-1/2/3.md`, `worker/worker-rules.md`) already encode this; no rule change needed.

## Open (tracked as issue)

Residual weak point observed this session: a sub-step's size must fit the EXECUTING worker's REMAINING context, not merely be "a sub-stage." Stage 1A was dispatched to a worker already at 24% (post-investigation + addendum) and died mid-stage uncommitted — the recap-after-stage clean-checkpoint guarantee failed because no commit landed before death. Candidate refinements (undecided): (a) pre-dispatch context-budget check before giving an implementation Go to a reused worker; (b) default-separate the investigation worker from the implementation worker (fresh worker for implementation, investigation report carried by Opus); (c) sub-step sizing keyed to remaining context. To decide later.

The proxy span-derivation port is implemented on dev: all 8 proxy modification passes record position-anchored ops (`_all_ops` in `apply_modification_rules`, `src/proxy/rules.py`), and the production consumer (`_build_stripped_injected_deltas`, `src/proxy/logging.py`) composes spans via `compose_block` (`src/proxy/diff_engine.py`). ## Task — Stage 3: codify the invariant as a CI-runnable regression test

The design intent (from `decisions/OldThemes/proxy_tool_stripping/16_operation_transcript_redesign.md`): the reconstruction invariant `original + ops == forwarded` is the GUARANTEE that production never silently mutates without recording an op. It must live as a CI/regression test, NOT a runtime fallback. A future pass that modifies content without recording an op must be caught automatically by this test.

This is an investigation-first task — there are real design questions:

1. **The probe is the basis.** Read `dev/proxy_dual_log/composition_probe.py` — `run_corpus`, `check_invariants`, `run_passes_and_collect_ops`, `compose_block`, `LOG_STEMS`. It already does the invariant check. Stage 3 turns it into a proper regression test with a clear pass/fail (exit code) runnable in CI.

2. **The test-corpus question (the crux).** The probe runs over `LOG_STEMS` in `src/logs/dual_log/`. Determine: are those `.jsonl` logs COMMITTED or gitignored/ephemeral runtime data? A CI/regression test needs a STABLE, committed corpus — it cannot depend on transient runtime logs. Propose the approach: e.g. commit a small representative corpus fixture (a subset of real captured entries covering the key cases — multi-pass, double-inject/dedup, TN-transform, the money-shot) under `dev/proxy_dual_log/fixtures/`, OR run against available logs with a graceful skip when absent. Drei Stück:

**1. Directives read-side.** CC schickt im Request-Body zwei Direktiven mit, `context_management` und `diagnostics`. Der Proxy *erfasst* sie schon (die Write-Seite trägt sie in einer früheren Session ins `_forwarded`-Log), aber sie werden im Proxy-Pane **noch nicht gerendert**. Read-Side = sie im REQUEST-Teil des Proxy-Panes anzeigen. Blockiert auf echte Einträge (braucht Restart + Traffic, damit `_forwarded`-Einträge sie tatsächlich tragen).

**2. Token-Pane-Nits** — zwei kleine Fixes in der Token-Pane:
- `overage:rejected(org_level_disabled)` wird bei *jedem* Request angezeigt — das ist eine Dauer-Account-Eigenschaft (deine Org hat Overage deaktiviert), trägt null Per-Request-Signal, also Rauschen. Unterdrücken.
- Der `float(utilization)`-Parse in `format_cache_tracker` ist ungeschützt — wenn der Rate-Limit-Utilization-Header mal kein sauberer Float-String ist, crasht es. Geringes Risiko (ist zuverlässig ein Float-String), aber absichern.

**3. thinking_tokens-consumed** (deferred, der harte). Die Token-Pane zeigt das *angeforderte* Thinking-Budget (`think:Nk`), nicht die tatsächlich *verbrauchten* Reasoning-Tokens. Der Verbrauchswert steht nicht in der Session-JSONL — nur im rohen SSE-Response-Stream, den der Proxy ohne Buffering durchreicht. Den zu holen bräuchte Proxy-SSE-Buffering (bricht das Stream-Pass-Design). Darum aufgeschoben.

Alle drei sind unabhängig vom Port, den wir gerade fertig gemacht haben. Sie hängen in #12, weil ich #10 reingefaltet habe.

Ich fasse zusammen, was wir in #10 angehen — und wo die Grenze zwischen "schon erledigt" und "jetzt dran" liegt.

Die **Write-Seite ist laut Issue schon fertig und auf dev gemerged**: `_forwarded` trägt jetzt `anthropic_beta`, und ein neuer `_response` Dual-Log fängt in `responseheaders()` die Rate-Limit-Header-Familie plus Request-ID für alle Status-Codes ab. Das Einzige, was hier noch offen ist, ist ein **Live-Verify** — der braucht einen Proxy-Restart, weil die Live-Copy aktuell eingefroren ist.

**Was wir jetzt tatsächlich bauen, ist die Read-Seite und das Pane-Redesign:**

Erstens das **Surfacing von Feldern, die schon geloggt aber noch nirgends angezeigt werden** — auf Session-JSONL-Seite die Usage-Extras (cache_creation 5m/1h-Split, server_tool_use, service_tier, speed, iterations, inference_geo), auf Request-Seite die Direktiven context_management/diagnostics, und auf Response-Seite die Rate-Limit-Header.

Zweitens das **komplette Pane-Redesign in zwei Blöcke**, strukturiert entlang REQUEST und RESPONSE, sodass das Proxy-Pane die visuelle Single-Source-of-Truth wird.

Ich habe mich eingelesen — RAG plus die echten Code-Pfade plus die Logs auf der Platte. Zwei Sachen vorweg, die wichtiger sind als die Roadmap selbst, weil sie von dem abweichen, was in unseren Docs steht:

**Die Write-Seite läuft nicht nur, sie ist faktisch schon live-verifiziert.** Das Issue sagt „live-verify pending proxy restart, frozen live-copy" — das stimmt nicht mehr. Auf der Platte liegen echte `_response`-Logs und `_forwarded`-Einträge mit `anthropic_beta`. Ich habe einen echten Response-Header-Satz angeschaut: Status 200, die Rate-Limit-Header sind da. Der Live-Verify-Punkt ist damit erledigt.

**Aber: Die Rate-Limit-Header sehen völlig anders aus als unsere Reference-Docs annehmen.** Die Anthropic-Doku (und unser OldThemes) beschreibt die klassische API-Familie `anthropic-ratelimit-{requests,tokens}-{limit,remaining,reset}`. Auf unserem OAuth-/Abo-Traffic kommt stattdessen die **`anthropic-ratelimit-unified-*`-Familie** zurück: `unified-5h-utilization`, `unified-5h-reset`, `unified-7d-utilization`, `unified-7d-reset`, `unified-representative-claim`, Overage-Status. Das ist das Subscription-Modell mit 5-Stunden- und 7-Tage-Fenstern, nicht das Per-Minute-Token-Modell. **Das ändert das Design des RESPONSE-Blocks materiell** — wir zeigen 5h/7d-Auslastung in Prozent und Reset-Zeitpunkt, nicht „Tokens remaining".

# Context (architecture — read this, it governs every decision)

This project monitors Claude Code API traffic through a mitmproxy. There are two display panes, and a hard architectural boundary separates them:

- **Proxy pane = REQUEST only.** It shows what the proxy sends TO the API for request N (request body + request headers). Data captured by the proxy on the outbound wire.
- **Token pane = RESPONSE.** It shows what the server returns for request N. (NOT your task — do not touch it.)

Your task is the **Proxy-pane (request) cluster** of a larger observability feature. A separate later task handles the token-pane side.

# Your task (four pieces)

## 1. Write-side carry: `context_management` + `diagnostics` into the forwarded delta
In `src/proxy/logging.py`, `_build_forwarded_delta(payload, ...)` builds the per-request `_forwarded` dual-log entry. It currently carries scalars `max_tokens` and `output_config` from the payload. Add two more request-body fields the same way: `context_management` and `diagnostics`, pulled directly from `payload`.

- These are real fields Claude Code sends in the request body. Verified real shapes from our logs:
  - `context_management`: `{"edits": [{"type": "clear_thinking_20251015", "keep": "all"}]}` (often absent)
  - `diagnostics`: `{"previous_message_id": null}` (often present, value often null)
- Mirror the existing `output_config` handling — `payload.get("context_management")` / `payload.get("diagnostics")`, carry whatever is there (None/absent is fine).
- NOTE the contrast: `anthropic_beta` was added in `src/proxy/addon.py` (lines ~149-156) because it's an HTTP *header* not in the body payload. `context_management`/`diagnostics` ARE body fields, so they belong directly in `_build_forwarded_delta` in logging.py — read addon.py only to understand the pattern, do not add these two there.
- **This session we do NOT read or render the directives** — only the write-side carry, so future logs contain them. No parser/render work for context_management/diagnostics.

## 2. Parser: extract `anthropic_beta`
In `src/proxy_display/parser.py`, `_extract_forwarded_fields(fwd_entry, ...)` builds the display entry dict from a forwarded entry. The forwarded entry already carries `anthropic_beta` (a list of CC beta-feature flags, varies per request — 6/9/13-flag subsets). Pull it onto the entry, e.g. `entry['anthropic_beta'] = fwd_entry.get('anthropic_beta') or []`. Real sample value: `['oauth-2025-04-20', 'interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14', 'redact-thinking-2026-02-12', 'thinking-token-count-2026-05-13', 'context-management-2025-06-27', 'prompt-caching-scope-2026-01-05']`.

## 3. Render: new `beta:` drill-down section
In the proxy pane's expanded per-request view, add a new collapsible `beta:` section showing the flag list, mirroring the existing drill-down sections (`render_system_blocks`, `render_tools`, `render_fields_delta` in `src/proxy_display/render_sections.py`, wired into the expanded block of `render_turn_expanded` in `src/proxy_display/render_turn.py`). A collapsed line like `beta: 6 flags` expanding to the list is the intent. Follow the exact expand/collapse + key pattern the existing sections use.

## 4. Render: remove CR/CC from the proxy pane header row
The proxy pane request header row currently shows `CR:.. CC:..` (in `render_turn.py` around lines 105-121, the `cr_cc_str`; also in `src/proxy_display/render_entry.py`, the no-turns path). These are RESPONSE data (cache read/creation, joined from the Session-JSONL) and violate the proxy-pane=request boundary — remove them from the header. The token pane owns CR/CC. Keep the request-side budget indicators (`eff:`, `think:`) — those are request data and stay.

## 2026-06-08 — Architecture refinement (the request/response boundary)

The earlier "two-block REQUEST/RESPONSE inside the proxy pane" plan was rejected. Two principles replace it.

**1. Pane assignment by request/response semantics.** Each pane is indexed per request N, but each shows a different direction of that exchange:
- **Proxy pane = REQUEST** — what the proxy sends TO the API for req N (request body + request headers). The response of req N feeds into req N+1; the design counts in requests, not separate blocks. We do NOT restructure into REQUEST/RESPONSE blocks — we enrich the request-centric rows with request-side fields only.
- **Token pane = RESPONSE** — what the server returns for req N. Since req N's response is not re-sent to the API in req N+1, it belongs to the token pane, not the proxy pane.

**2. Two orthogonal questions — do not conflate:**
- *What may the proxy LOG?* → **wire-Lesart**: only data the proxy captures on the wire. Request body/headers (`_original`/`_forwarded`) ✅; response HEADERS (`_response`, read in `responseheaders()` before the body streams) ✅; response BODY/usage object ❌ (2xx `stream=True` pass-through — never touches the proxy; CC writes it to Session-JSONL). `_response` capture stays as-is, it is legitimate.
- *Which pane DISPLAYS a datum?* → request/response semantics, independent of which file logged it. The rate-limit headers are wire-captured by the proxy (so the `_response` log is correct) but are RESPONSE data → displayed in the **token pane**. The token pane therefore reads TWO sources for req N's response: the Session-JSONL usage object + the proxy `_response` rate-limit headers.

**Consequences:**
- Proxy pane loses CR/CC from the header (response data; token pane owns it) — restores the boundary (CR/CC were a long-standing leak of Session-JSONL response data into the request pane).
- Proxy pane gains only request-side enrichment: `beta:` (request header) and — once read-side lands — `context_management`/`diagnostics` (request body directives, write-carried into `_forwarded` this session).
- The `usage:`-section-in-the-proxy-pane idea is DEAD — usage extras are Session-JSONL response data → token pane.
- Rate-limit `ratelimit:` rendering moves from proxy pane → token pane.

## 2026-06-08 — Response-header family CORRECTION (unified, not classic)

The rate-limit family on our OAuth/subscription traffic is NOT the classic API `anthropic-ratelimit-{requests,tokens,input-tokens,output-tokens}-{limit,remaining,reset}` the reference docs (and `## Response-Header rate-limit family` below) describe. Real captured `_response` headers (status 200, verified on disk 2026-06-08) are the **`anthropic-ratelimit-unified-*` subscription family**: `unified-status`, `unified-5h-{status,reset,utilization}`, `unified-7d-{status,reset,utilization}`, `unified-representative-claim`, `unified-fallback-percentage`, `unified-reset`, `unified-overage-{status,disabled-reason}`, plus `request-id`, `anthropic-organization-id`. (`anthropic-priority-*`/`anthropic-fast-*` absent on OAuth traffic.) The write-side filter `_filter_response_headers` already captures these via the `anthropic-ratelimit-` prefix match. Token-pane RESPONSE rendering targets the unified 5h/7d utilization + reset — NOT requests/tokens-remaining. This is exactly the "what really happens vs. what the API docs say" gap the header capture was built to close.

Konkret. Original-Content:
```
Hello <system-reminder>foo</system-reminder> world <bg-output>junk</bg-output> done
```
- Pass A (SR-Strip) entfernt `<system-reminder>foo</system-reminder>` → `Hello  world <bg-output>junk</bg-output> done`
- Pass B (bg-exit) entfernt `<bg-output>junk</bg-output>` und injiziert `[wakeup]` → `Hello  world [wakeup] done` ← das ist forwarded

Die korrekte Span-Liste über dem **Original** muss sein:
```
"Hello "                                  equal  (grau)
"<system-reminder>foo</system-reminder>"  stripped (gelb)
" world "                                 equal

"[wakeup]"                                injected (grün)  ← nicht im Original, sitzt zwischen zwei equal-Ankern im forwarded
" done"                                   equal
```

Der Knackpunkt: Pass B hat seinen Strip an einem Offset im **Post-Pass-A-Content** gefunden — nicht im Original. Pass A hat aber davor schon Bytes entfernt, also ist jeder Offset von Pass B gegen das Original **verschoben**. Um den bg-output-Strip an der richtigen Stelle im Original zu platzieren, muss sein Offset durch Pass As Entfernung **zurück-gerechnet** (rebased) werden. Verrechnest du dich um *ein* Byte, fängt das gelbe Feld einen Tick zu früh oder zu spät an — und blutet ins `" world"` oder `" done"`. Genau diese Boundary-Verschiebung ist die Quelle der hässlichen Färbungen.

„Byte-genau komponieren" heißt also: der Algorithmus, der die Per-Pass-Operationen zu einer Span-Liste über dem Original zusammenrechnet, trifft **jede** Grenze auf das exakte Byte — beweisbar durch zwei Checks: (1) die Operationen rekonstruieren den forwarded Content byte-identisch, und (2) jedes Original-Byte ist korrekt als equal-oder-stripped klassifiziert, jedes forwarded-Byte das nicht im Original war als injected.

# Background — the problem (Layer diagnosis)
The proxy strips/injects content in API request messages. A separate derived log (`_stripped`/`_injected` dual-logs) records WHAT was stripped/injected, and the monitor renders it as a yellow/green overlay. Two confirmed bugs (double-rendered injection; phantom green on unchanged content) both live in the WRITE side — the derivation of that span log — NOT in the actual proxy send (correct) nor the renderer (faithful).

Root cause: the current recording stores text CHUNKS keyed by message index (`pass_removed_by_idx[idx] = [chunks]` in `src/proxy/rules.py`), with NO position and NO operation order. The span builder (`build_message_spans` / `_diff_text` in `src/proxy/diff_engine.py`) then re-SEARCHES for chunk positions at build time — that search double-counts and mis-aligns.

# The new model we are validating
Record each modification as a POSITION-ANCHORED operation `(block_idx, offset_in_inner_content, removed_text, injected_text)` at the moment the pass acts, then COMPOSE all operations across passes into one span list over the original — deterministically, no diff, no dedup, no fallback.

# The CORE this probe must prove — multi-pass composition, byte-exact
Messages go through MULTIPLE sequential passes; each pass operates on the OUTPUT of the previous one, so each pass's offsets are relative to the already-modified content, NOT the original. To build the span list over the ORIGINAL, each pass's operation must be rebased through all prior passes' edits. Getting this off by one byte = yellow/green bleeds into adjacent content.

Worked example. Original:
`Hello <system-reminder>foo</system-reminder> world <bg-output>junk</bg-output> done`
- Pass A (SR strip) removes `<system-reminder>foo</system-reminder>`
- Pass B (bg-exit) removes `<bg-output>junk</bg-output>` and injects `[wakeup]`
- Forwarded: `Hello  world [wakeup] done`

Correct span list over the ORIGINAL:
```
"Hello "                                  equal
"<system-reminder>foo</system-reminder>"  stripped
" world "                                 equal
"<bg-output>junk</bg-output>"             stripped
"[wakeup]"                                injected   (between equal anchors in forwarded; not in original)
" done"                                   equal
```
Pass B recorded its offset in the POST-pass-A content; placing the bg-output strip correctly in the original requires rebasing through pass A's removal.

# Two invariant checks the probe must run on every case
1. **Reconstruction:** applying the composed operations to the original content reproduces the forwarded content BYTE-IDENTICAL.
2. **Classification:** every original byte is correctly equal-or-stripped; every forwarded byte not present in the original is injected. No off-by-one, no bleed.

# Your probe (build in dev/proxy_dual_log/)

(Per-pass before→after is a single, unambiguous transformation — distinct from diffing the whole collapsed original→forwarded, which is the thing we are eliminating.)
3. **Composition algorithm (the core):** merge the per-pass operations into one span list over C0, rebasing offsets across passes. This is the heart — design it carefully.

Standard: a code path that, on missing input or failure, SILENTLY produces alternative output through a second method — a fallback — is the highest-severity debugging liability. When two routes can produce the "same" output by different means, a wrong result looks plausible and you cannot tell which route made it. Symptom: you fix one case (whack the mole) and a different case breaks (next mole), because patches (dedup, gating, "best-effort" branches) accrete ON TOP of the fallback instead of removing it. The root data is usually computed once correctly upstream; the fallback re-derives it lossily downstream.

Distinguish from a **tripwire/assertion** — a check that REFUSES to produce output and surfaces the failure (raise / flag / render-plain-with-marker). That is NOT a violation; it is the cure. The violation is specifically the route that GUESSES an alternative output to keep going.

Heuristic — manual review per hit (like 2.6a); some flagged constructs are legitimate (a cache-miss returning `None` is a tripwire, not a fallback). The judgment per hit: does the flagged branch PRODUCE derived output by a second method, or does it REFUSE and surface?

Textual signatures:
```bash
# markers in comments and names
grep -rniE '\b(fall ?back|legacy path|old path|best.?effort|backward.?compat)\b' src \
  --include='*.py' | grep -vE '/(logs|worktrees|__pycache__)/'
grep -rnE 'def _?\w*(fallback|legacy|dedup|gated)\w*\(' src \
  --include='*.py' | grep -vE '/(logs|worktrees|__pycache__)/'
```

Structural signature — `except` that returns a value without re-raising (silent degradation):
```python
# /tmp/refactor_silent_except.py
import ast, os
SKIP = ('__pycache__', '/logs/', '/worktrees/', '/dev/', '/tests/')
hits = []
for root, _, files in os.walk('src'):
    if any(x in root for x in SKIP):
        continue
    for fn in files:
        if not fn.endswith('.py'):
            continue
        p = os.path.join(root, fn)
        try:
            tree = ast.parse(open(p).read())
        except SyntaxError:
            continue
        for n in ast.walk(tree):
            if not isinstance(n, ast.Try):
                continue
            for h in n.handlers:
                produces = any(isinstance(x, ast.Return) and x.value is not None
                               for x in ast.walk(h))
                reraises = any(isinstance(x, ast.Raise) for x in ast.walk(h))
                if produces and not reraises:
                    hits.append((p, h.lineno))
for p, l in hits:
    print(f"  {p}:{l}  except returns a value, no re-raise — silent fallback?")
print(f"\nTotal: {len(hits)} (review each: fallback that guesses vs tripwire that refuses)")
```

Structural signature — sentinel branch feeding two derivations of one output (`if <key> in x: <new path>  else: <old path>`): not reliably AST-detectable without per-project knowledge. Surface it during manual review of the textual hits, and during any "why are there two ways to compute X" reading of the codebase.

**This dimension does NOT auto-produce a refactor candidate.** A silent-fallback finding is architectural — its fix is a one-way redesign that MUST be evaluated WITH the user. Route every hit to the One-Way Redesign Evaluation companion below.

Unlike LOC, mutation, or coupling findings — which a worker refactors mechanically — a silent-fallback finding (2.8) cannot be auto-fixed. Removing a fallback safely requires rebuilding the strategy so a SINGLE deterministic route produces the output, with correctness guaranteed structurally rather than guarded at runtime. That redesign is architectural and is evaluated WITH the user. The scan surfaces candidates; this companion is the evaluation process.

**Per candidate, first classify (the decisive distinction):**
- **Fallback** (eliminate): primary route fails / input missing → produce alternative output by a second method. Hides the failure behind plausible-but-wrong output. This is the debugging hell.
- **Tripwire / assertion** (keep, shaped right): check a property; on violation REFUSE to produce output and surface it. Never a second derivation. Leave it (or harden it to refuse-and-surface).

**One-way redesign — work through with the user:**
1. **Record once at the source.** Can the data be captured at the point of truth (where the operation actually happens) with enough information — position, identity, order — that a single deterministic path produces the output later, with no re-derivation or inference downstream? The downstream re-derivation is almost always the fallback's home.
2. **Completeness is a CODE property, not an INPUT property.** Operations happen at a finite, enumerable set of code sites. New input can only trigger an existing (recorded) site or no operation at all — it cannot manufacture an unrecorded one. So completeness can be VERIFIED exhaustively, not hoped for at runtime.
3. **Move the safety check from runtime to test.** Replace the runtime fallback with a test-time invariant: `source + recorded operations == produced output`, asserted over a real corpus and kept as a CI regression test. A failure there = a code site that forgot to record = fix the site. The test IS the guarantee; it catches future code that forgets to record.
4. **Production runs one way.** After (1)–(3): one deterministic route, no fallback, no dedup-patch, no "best-effort". Any retained tripwire refuses-and-surfaces; it never guesses.

**Validate in `dev/` before touching `src/`.** The redesign is an architectural rewrite — build it as a `dev/` probe, prove exact equivalence on real data across ALL operation types (the invariant holds for every case), THEN port to `src/` and delete the fallback chain. Do not modify `src/` during the exploration (dev/-first rule, `~/.claude/shared-rules/global/documentation.md`).

**Anti-pattern — the self-defeating hedge:** prove the one-way path in `dev/` and then STILL ship a runtime fallback "just in case". That admits you distrust the proof, and it re-introduces the exact hell you set out to remove. If the `dev/` proof holds over the corpus and the invariant lives in CI, production needs no fallback — at most a refuse-and-surface tripwire for genuinely-novel input.
