Wenn Opus selbst nicht genau weiß was sich ändern muss → Plan-Pflicht.

### Sequential Sub-Stage Decomposition

**Plan once for the whole; execute one stage at a time with per-stage Opus sign-off.** When a task's implementation has interconnected, nested, build-on-each-other parts, a single monolithic "Go, build it all" dispatch OVERHEATS the worker — one huge thinking turn, token blowout, context exhausted mid-build, death with the whole thing in-flight and uncommitted. Splitting prevents this AND makes death recoverable by design.

**Two-part discipline:**

1. **The PLAN is full and upfront.** The worker's Phase-2 report decomposes the ENTIRE task into ordered stages — the stages are interconnected, so the worker needs the whole picture to plan coherently. Do NOT fragment the planning. (This is the normal plan-first from § Task Complexity → Plan or Go; here the plan additionally names the stage sequence.)

2. **The EXECUTION is fed one stage at a time, each with Opus sign-off.** After convergence on the plan, dispatch ONLY Stage 1 ("implement Stage 1, commit, report"). Worker implements → commits → reports → Opus reviews that stage (Phase-4-light: diff + verify) → ONLY THEN dispatch Stage 2. Never "Go, build the whole plan." Each stage is a small, independently-committable, verifiable unit.

**A stage = one coherent committable unit**, sized so the worker finishes it in a bounded turn without a context blowout. Examples: the single-pass core before the multi-pass composition; the data extractor before its consumer; one file of a multi-file refactor; one pass migrated before the next.

**Why this makes death recoverable by design:** the explicit staged plan + the per-stage commits ARE the successor map. A dying worker leaves "plan says next is Stage N, last commit = Stage N-1" — the successor resumes at exactly that coordinate with NO archaeology of where death happened. Contrast the monolith: a worker that dies mid-build leaves one half-done in-flight task that must be reverse-engineered. Staged execution turns Worker Death Recovery (workers-3.md § Worker Death Recovery) from forensic reconstruction into a plan-index lookup.

**Interlocks with:**
- § Worker Phase 5 Recap (workers-2.md) — recap-after-every-stage already commits clean state per stage; this rule is its dispatch-side complement.
- § AGGRESSIVE REUSE / successor-from-checkpoint (workers-3.md) — staged commits are the checkpoints a successor resumes from.

**Trigger:** any substantial implementation — multi-file changes, algorithm/probe builds, refactors spanning several units, architectural ports. If the implementation has natural sequential stages OR would burn the worker through a large fraction of its context in one turn, stage it. Single-file known fixes (§ Task Complexity straightforward) need no staging.

Status: **architecture validated on real data by dev/ probe; src/ port PENDING (staged).** Supersedes the patch-on-patch approach of the `_diff_text` fallback + `_dedup_wakeup_blocks` for the `_stripped`/`_injected` span construction.

## Origin

Two live proxy-pane bugs (issue #5 bundle), both surfaced 2026-06-08:
- **Green-overlay false-injection** — unchanged content rendered green (injected). Documented arc: `14_green_overlay_false_injection.md`.
- **bg-exit double-inject** — `_WAKEUP_TEXT` rendered green TWICE on a single bg-exit block (user screenshot "doppelt der inj gerendert").

## Layer diagnosis (the key reframing)

An API exchange through the proxy has three layers. Mapping the bugs to a layer settled the whole approach:

| Layer | What it is | State |
|---|---|---|
| 1. What the proxy actually SENDS | the forwarded payload | **correct** — verified: bg-exit forwarded content carries `_WAKEUP_TEXT` exactly once (per-message count = 1 across the corpus) |
| 2. What gets WRITTEN to the logs | the derived `_stripped`/`_injected` span deltas | **WRONG** — double-records the injection / phantom-tags unchanged content |
| 3. What the monitor SHOWS | the renderer | **correct** — faithfully renders whatever spans Layer 2 wrote |

Both bugs live in **Layer 2** — the derived span log. Layer 1 (the real send) is right; Layer 3 (render) faithfully shows wrong Layer-2 data. The renderer was never the bug (confirmed in `14`).

## Why the current Layer-2 derivation fails

The passes record text CHUNKS keyed by message index (`pass_removed_by_idx[idx] = [chunks]` in `rules.py`) — **no position, no operation order**. The span builder (`build_message_spans` / `_diff_text` in `diff_engine.py`) then RE-SEARCHES for chunk positions at build time (`fwd_text.find(chunk)`). That search is the bug surface: it finds the same chunk twice (double-inject) or, when no chunk is recorded, falls back to word-level `_diff_text` which mis-tags a shared JSON-structural prefix as injected (phantom). `_dedup_wakeup_blocks` is a patch on top of the double; the diff-fallback is a patch on top of the missing record. Patch-on-patch: fix one mole, the next pops up.

## The new model — operation transcript

Record each modification as a POSITION-ANCHORED operation at the moment the pass acts; compose all operations across passes into one span list over the original — deterministically. Three pillars:

1. **Position-anchored op records**, not text chunks. Each modification site emits `(block_idx, offset, removed_text, injected_text)`. The function knows the position — it found its target via regex at a concrete offset.
2. **Deterministic composition, no diff/dedup/fallback.** Compose the per-pass ops into a span list over the original. No re-search, no post-hoc dedup — each op is recorded once with its position.
3. **Reconstruction invariant as a TEST (not a runtime net).** `original + recorded ops == forwarded`, asserted over a real corpus, kept as a CI regression test.

## No runtime fallback — the decisive design stance

The user's hard constraint (and correct): the current bugs ARE caused by the fallbacks (`_diff_text`, `_dedup`). Adding a new runtime fallback repeats the mistake. Resolution:

- **Completeness is a CODE property, not an INPUT property.** Modifications happen at a finite, enumerable set of code sites. New input can only trigger an EXISTING (recorded) site or no modification (correctly equal). Input cannot manufacture an unrecorded modification. So completeness is verifiable EXHAUSTIVELY, not hoped for.
- The reconstruction invariant lives in **dev/ + CI test**, not production. A failure there = a code site that mutates without recording = fix the site. The test IS the guarantee; it catches future code that forgets to record.
- **Production runs one way.** Deterministic composition from records. No `_diff_text`, no `_dedup`, no "best-effort". The only residual case — a future code change adding a mutation without a record — is caught by the CI invariant, NOT a runtime branch.
- **Fallback vs tripwire (the line that matters):** a *fallback* produces alternative output by a second method (hides failure behind plausible-wrong output) → eliminate. A *tripwire/assertion* refuses to produce output and surfaces the failure → legitimate, never guesses. The redesign keeps zero fallbacks; any retained check is a tripwire.
- **Anti-pattern named:** proving the one-way path in dev/ and then STILL shipping a runtime fallback "just in case" = distrusting the proof = re-introducing the hell. If the dev/ proof holds and the invariant is in CI, production needs no fallback.

(This stance was generalized into the refactor skill: `Meta/iterative-dev/.../iterative-dev-refactor/SKILL.md` § 2.8 Silent-Fallback Scan + § One-Way Redesign Evaluation companion.)

## Composition algorithm + proof (dev/ probe, validated)

Algorithm (worker `composition-probe`): per block a span list `[(tag, text)]`, `tag ∈ {equal, stripped, injected}`, initialized `[("equal", C0)]`. Two invariants maintained after every op:
- `equal+stripped == C0` (original reconstruction)
- `equal+injected == Ck` (current content)

Applying op `(offset_in_Ck, removed, injected)`: walk the list tracking a Ck-cursor (stripped spans don't advance it); equal bytes in the removal range → stripped; injected bytes in the removal range → disappear; insert `("injected", injected)` at the splice point. The dual invariant solves the multi-pass offset-rebasing implicitly — the span list carries original AND current content simultaneously, so each new op's Ck-offset maps onto the structure without explicit rebasing.

**`_dedup_wakeup_blocks` is a Layer-1 payload op** (it changes what gets forwarded — Cfwd has one wakeup), modeled as a composed op `Op(offset_2nd, wakeup, '')`, NOT a span-building hack. There is no separate dedup in the span path.

**Proof** (dev/proxy_dual_log/composition_probe.py + 01_reports/composition_probe_20260608.md): **7219/7219 blocks byte-exact** across 484 modified requests / 492 entries / 5 stems. 676 multi-pass blocks, 480 double-inject blocks — all pass both invariants. Per-pass-type: first_pass 5499, cumulative_sr 1030, bg_exit 480, dedup_wakeup 480, po_preview 373, hook_prefix 320, final_sr 75 — all 100%. Money shot msg[100] (TN+BG double): composes to exactly ONE injected wakeup, C0(406)+Cfwd(48) byte-exact → double-inject FIXED by the model.

The report's **Op-Shape-Per-Pass table is the src/ port spec** — what each pass must record directly instead of the probe's `(before,after)` stand-in.

## Open (for the src/ port)

- **Staged port** (per workers-1.md § Sequential Sub-Stage Decomposition): pass-by-pass precise recording → composition builder to src/ → invariant as CI test → remove `_diff_text` fallback + `_dedup` span-patch. One stage at a time with per-stage sign-off.
- **sidecar / idle_recap UNVERIFIED** — absent from the 5-stem corpus. Theoretical model: `Op(0,0,full_original,marker)` single full-replacement, trivially composable. Port must close with real data.
- **TN-transform recording is the trickiest** — `_apply_first_pass` TN branch strips the XML wrapper while keeping inner text (not a clean strip); its op is `(prefix_len, changed_region, new_region)`, not a clean chunk removal.

## Why this exists

Recurring terminology collision: "hooks" in this repo refers to TWO unrelated
systems that both ride CC's hook mechanism. The state file the worker-status
machinery reads is literally named `hooks.json`, which sounds like it belongs to
the `src/hooks/` safety system — it does not. This file fixes the vocabulary so
future discussion names the right system.

## Definition

A **hook** is an event-based mechanism: when event E fires, CC runs handler H
(`python3 <script>`, registered in `~/.claude/settings.json`). Two orthogonal
axes describe any hook in this repo:

1. **Event class** — WHAT fires the hook:
   - **Tool events** (`PreToolUse` / `PostToolUse`) — fire around every tool call.
   - **Session-lifecycle events** (`UserPromptSubmit` / `Stop` / `StopFailure`) — fire around conversation turns.
   Both event classes are **CC-standard** — they always exist. You opt in by
   registering a handler. Registering a handler for `UserPromptSubmit` does not
   make the event custom; the EVENT is standard, only the HANDLER is ours.

2. **Handler purpose** — WHAT the script does:
   - **Safety / control** — block (exit 2) or rewrite (exit 0 + `updatedInput`) tool inputs.
   - **Activity tracking** — record session working/idle state.

"Custom" always refers to the HANDLER (our script), never the EVENT.

## The two systems

| | Tool-Hooks (Safety) | Activity-Hooks |
|---|---|---|
| Events | `PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse` | `UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `StopFailure` |
| Handler | `src/hooks/*.py` (22 PreToolUse + 1 PostToolUse; 28 total registrations across Bash/Edit/Read matchers) | `src/menubar/hook_writer.py` (one script, all three events) |
| Does what | Intercept Bash/Edit/Read → block destructive patterns / rewrite broken inputs | Track turn boundaries → write current session status |
| Output | `src/logs/hook_firing.jsonl` — append-forever fire LOG | app-support `hooks.json` — live STATE file (overwritten, not appended) |
| Installer | `src/hooks/hook_setup.py` | `src/menubar/hook_setup.py` |
| Consumers | FP-analysis / audit (`jq` over the jsonl) | menubar `proc_cache.py` (reads every 1s, status display + auto-abort); iterative-dev `worker-cli status` |

## The activity `hooks.json` state file

NOT a log. A ~1KB live state map, one entry per CC session:

```json
"af20354b-...": { "status": "idle", "cwd": ".../cost-sweep", "updated_ts": 1780874843 }
```

- `UserPromptSubmit` fires at turn-start → `hook_writer.py` sets `status: working`.
- `Stop` / `StopFailure` fires at turn-end → sets `status: idle`.
- Overwritten each turn; only the latest state survives.
- `hook_writer.py` also delivers queued menubar messages from `msg_queue.json` on Stop (separate feature, same handler).

This file is the single authoritative "is session X working or idle" source. The
menubar reads it; `worker-cli status` reads the same file. When the project says
"worker-cli orients on the menubar," it means THIS file — not the safety-hook
fire log.

## Naming collision (the trap)

`hooks.json` (activity state) ⟂ `src/hooks/` (safety scripts) ⟂ `hook_firing.jsonl`
(safety fire log). Three "hook" names, two unrelated systems. The activity state
file predates the `src/hooks/` safety suite's naming and was never renamed.

## Convention going forward

- **"Tool-Hooks"** = `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse` safety scripts in `src/hooks/` → `hook_firing.jsonl`.
- **"Activity-Hooks"** = `UserPromptSubmit`/`Stop`/`StopFailure` handler `src/menubar/hook_writer.py` → state file `hooks.json`.
- **"hooks.json"** unqualified = the activity state file. The safety fire log is always called by its full name `hook_firing.jsonl`.

## Surfaced in

Session investigating `worker-cli status` returning `unknown`: worker-cli reads
the activity `hooks.json` for status, broke when the menubar's app-support dir was
renamed (`com.brunowinter.monitor_cc_menubar` → `monitor-cc-menubar`) and worker-cli
kept the old underscore path. Root cause is path divergence in the activity-hook
system, entirely unrelated to the `src/hooks/` safety system.

# Task

`worker-cli status <name>` currently returns `unknown` for every worker. The status-detection
logic lives in ONE file: `src/spawn/tmux_spawn.sh`, function `_worker_detect_status` (and its
two callers `worker_status` / `worker_list`). Fix it so worker-cli reports the worker's real
status by reading the menubar's activity-status file directly, with no heuristic fallback layers.

## Background you need (provided — do NOT search for this, do NOT use rag-cli or web)

**The activity-status file.** The Monitor_CC menubar writes a small live state file per CC
session. worker-cli reads it to answer `status`. Its current canonical location is:

```
~/Library/Application Support/com.brunowinter.monitor-cc-menubar/hooks.json
```

Note the bundle id uses HYPHENS: `monitor-cc-menubar`. Schema (one entry per session):

```json
"<session_id>": { "status": "working" | "idle", "cwd": "<path>", "updated_ts": <float> }
```

It is written by the menubar's CC lifecycle hooks: `UserPromptSubmit` sets `status: working` at
turn-start, `Stop`/`StopFailure` set `status: idle` at turn-end. It is a STATE file (overwritten
each turn), not a log. This is the single authoritative working/idle source — the menubar reads
the exact same file. (It is unrelated to the `src/hooks/` safety hooks of Monitor_CC; do not
conflate.)

**Root cause of the current `unknown`.** Two things, both in `tmux_spawn.sh`:

1. **Stale path (the trigger).** Line 178 hardcodes the OLD bundle id with UNDERSCORES
   (`com.brunowinter.monitor_cc_menubar`). The menubar renamed its app-support dir to HYPHENS
   a while ago; the old dir no longer holds `hooks.json`. So the `jq` read targets a non-existent
   file. The SAME stale underscore path is also on line 18 (`_ORCHESTRATOR_SIGNALS_FILE` —
   `orchestrator_signals.json`, the auto-abort signal-grace write target); that one is silently
   broken too and must be fixed to the hyphen path as well.

2. **`set -e` fragility (why it becomes `unknown` instead of the intended fallback).** The file
   sets `set -euo pipefail` at the top. Line 179 is a BARE assignment
   `hook_status=$(jq ... "$hook_file" 2>/dev/null)` — no `local`, no `|| true`. When `jq` exits
   non-zero (missing file → exit 2; `2>/dev/null` hides stderr but NOT the exit code), `set -e`
   kills the subshell immediately — BEFORE the existing line-180 fallback (`[ -z "$hook_status" ]`
   → echo "idle") can run. The `worker-cli` wrapper catches the non-zero exit with `|| echo "unknown"`.
   Verified: `bash -c 'set -e; x=$(jq .a /nonexistent 2>/dev/null); echo after'` never prints
   `after`.

## Desired design (thin client — agreed with the user)

worker-cli must be a THIN CLIENT of the menubar's status file. Single source, no parallel
heuristics. Concretely, `_worker_detect_status` should resolve to:

- **`exited`** — keep the existing local process-liveness checks: `pane_dead == 1`, OR pane has no
  child processes, OR no `claude` descendant of the pane PID. This is an orthogonal fact (is the
  process alive) that is NOT in `hooks.json`, and the orchestration revive/successor flow needs it.
  KEEP this logic as-is.
- **`working` / `idle`** — read `hooks.json[session_id].status` and return it VERBATIM. No demote.
- **`unknown`** — honest signal meaning "the menubar has no status for this session": the session
  has no JSONL yet, OR there is no `hooks.json` entry for the session_id, OR the file is missing/
  unreadable. Must NOT crash the subshell — must cleanly return `unknown`.

**Remove** from the status path: the `window_activity` stale-working demote block (currently when
`hook_status == "working"`, it checks `now - window_activity > 10s` and demotes to `idle`). That
was a re-implementation of the menubar's own logic and is exactly the kind of drift we are killing.
After the change, a `working` hook status returns `working`, full stop. (The user explicitly
accepts that a context-limit-stuck worker whose Stop-hook never fired will read `working` — that is
the truthful reflection of the hook state, and a genuine process death is still caught by the
`exited` checks above.)

The session_id derivation (pane_current_path → CC-encode `/._` to `-` → newest `*.jsonl` in
`~/.claude/projects/<encoded>/` → basename) STAYS unchanged. Only the status computation changes.

## set -e robustness

Make every command-substitution in `_worker_detect_status` that can legitimately fail (jq on a
possibly-missing file; the `ls ... *.jsonl | head` pipe when no JSONL exists) survive under
`set -euo pipefail` so the function reaches its intended `unknown`/`idle`/`working` return instead
of aborting the subshell. Use `|| true` / `|| echo <default>` as appropriate. Do NOT remove the
`set -euo pipefail` line globally — other functions rely on it; fix the specific substitutions.

## Scope guards

- Touch ONLY `src/spawn/tmux_spawn.sh`. Do NOT modify the wrapper `bin/worker-cli` (the bug is not
  there) or any other file's logic.
- Do NOT add new features, flags, or status values beyond `working`/`idle`/`exited`/`unknown`.
- Do NOT introduce new fallback layers — the whole point is fewer sources, not more.

Ich halte parallel die Root-Cause + Thin-Client-Entscheidung im iterative-dev-OldThemes fest.", "type": "text"}, {"preview": {"content": "# worker-cli status — Thin-Client of menubar hooks.json

## Problem

`worker-cli status <name>` returned `unknown` (and `context_pct` returned `—%`) for
EVERY worker, regardless of real state. The status machinery lives entirely in
`src/spawn/tmux_spawn.sh` (`_worker_detect_status`, `worker_status`, `worker_list`).

## Root cause — path divergence + set -e crash

Two compounding faults, both in `tmux_spawn.sh`:

1. **App-support dir rename, not propagated.** The Monitor_CC menubar renamed its
   application-support directory bundle-id from `com.brunowinter.monitor_cc_menubar`
   (underscores) to `com.brunowinter.monitor-cc-menubar` (hyphens) and added a
   migration in its `paths.py`. `tmux_spawn.sh` still hardcoded the OLD underscore path
   in TWO places: line 178 (`hooks.json` status read) and line 18
   (`_ORCHESTRATOR_SIGNALS_FILE`, the auto-abort signal-grace WRITE target). The status
   read targeted a non-existent file; the menubar reads signals from the new path, so
   worker-cli's signal writes also went unseen — the auto-abort signal-grace was
   silently dead too. One rename broke two cross-repo couplings.

2. **`set -e` turned "file missing" into a crash.** `tmux_spawn.sh` runs under
   `set -euo pipefail`. Line 179 was a BARE assignment `hook_status=$(jq … "$hook_file"
   2>/dev/null)` — no `local`, no `|| true`. `jq` on a missing file exits 2 (the
   `2>/dev/null` hides stderr, not the exit code). Under `set -e`, a bare assignment
   whose command-substitution fails aborts the subshell IMMEDIATELY — before the
   line-180 fallback (`[ -z "$hook_status" ]`) could run. The `worker-cli` wrapper
   catches the non-zero exit with `|| echo "unknown"`. So missing-file → crash →
   `unknown`. (The `ls … *.jsonl | head` pipe on line 171 had the same latent fault
   under `pipefail`.)

## Decision — thin client, no fallback chains

The status logic had accreted parallel sensors (tmux `window_activity` demote, JSONL
mtime, a re-implementation of the menubar's own demote rule). These were nominally
"the same source as the menubar" but were independent re-derivations that drift apart
on any change — exactly what the path rename exposed.

Redesign: `worker-cli` is a THIN CLIENT of the menubar's activity-status file. Single
source, no heuristic layers.

- **`working` / `idle`** — `hooks.json[session_id].status` returned VERBATIM. No demote,
  no `window_activity`, no JSONL-mtime. The menubar's CC lifecycle hooks
  (`UserPromptSubmit`→working, `Stop`/`StopFailure`→idle) are the authority.
- **`exited`** — kept as a LOCAL, orthogonal process-liveness check (`pane_dead=1`, no
  child PIDs, no `claude` descendant). This is a fact not present in `hooks.json` and the
  revive/successor flow needs it. Not a fallback — an independent signal.
- **`unknown`** — honest verdict: hooks.json missing, no entry for session_id, no JSONL
  yet, or pane unreadable. Means "the menubar tells me nothing about this session" —
  one precise, debuggable meaning. NO guessed `idle` default.

Accepted trade-off: a worker stuck at the context limit (CC alive, `Stop` hook never
fired) reads `working` verbatim rather than being heuristically demoted to `idle`. That
is the truthful reflection of the hook state; a genuine process death is still caught
by the `exited` checks.

### unknown is a verdict, not an error (return 0)

All `unknown` paths originally did `echo "unknown"; return 1`. Combined with the
wrapper's `… || echo "unknown"` capture, the non-zero exit fired the `|| echo` ON TOP of
the already-printed verdict → `STATUS="unknown\nunknown"` (two lines). This broke
`context_pct`'s `[ "$status" = "unknown" ]` check (multiline mismatch → could read a
wrong %). Fix: all status paths return exit 0 (the echo IS the verdict, consistent with
`exited`/`working`/`idle`). The wrapper's `|| echo "unknown"` now only fires on genuine
crashes — a real safety net, not a duplicate.

## Path is a cross-repo contract

The lasting fragility is the hardcoded app-support path duplicated across two repos
(Monitor_CC menubar writes it, iterative-dev worker-cli reads it). The fix points both
`tmux_spawn.sh` references at the live hyphen path; that path must be treated as a frozen
contract — a future rename requires updating both sides together.

## Cross-reference

The activity-`hooks.json` consumed here is unrelated to Monitor_CC's `src/hooks/`
tool-safety hooks. The hook taxonomy (CC-standard lifecycle events vs custom tool hooks;
activity state file vs safety fire log) is documented in Monitor_CC
`decisions/OldThemes/hook_taxonomy.md`.

## Live verification (post-publish)

`worker-cli status status-fix` → `idle 68%` (was `unknown —%`). `worker-cli status --all`
single-line clean output. Cache (`plugin-publish`-synced) has 2 hyphen-path refs, 0
underscore refs.

**1. Die tmux-Session ist ganz weg.** `worker_status` prüft zuerst `tmux has-session`; schlägt das fehl → `unknown`. Das passiert, wenn ein Worker stirbt oder gekillt wird, ohne über `worker-cli kill` zu laufen (Crash, manuelles `tmux kill`, Reboot) und einen verwaisten Registry-Eintrag hinterlässt — genau der cost-sweep-Fall vorhin. Wichtig: das ist **nicht** dasselbe wie `exited`. `exited` heißt "Session lebt, aber der CC-Prozess im Pane ist tot" (pane_dead=1 oder kein claude-Child). `unknown` hier heißt "die Session selbst existiert nicht mehr".

**2. Frisch gespawnt, noch in Initialisierung.** In den ersten paar Sekunden nach dem Spawn, bevor CC seine Session-JSONL geschrieben hat (kein JSONL gefunden → unknown), bzw. bevor der erste `UserPromptSubmit`-Hook den Eintrag in `hooks.json` geschrieben hat (kein Hook-Eintrag → unknown). Das ist das ehrliche "läuft noch an" — kippt von selbst auf working/idle, sobald der erste Turn registriert ist.

**3. Die Menubar/Activity-Hooks liefern nichts.** `hooks.json` fehlt komplett, oder hat keinen Eintrag für diese session_id. Auf einer Maschine ohne installierte Activity-Hooks würde **jeder** Worker `unknown` liefern — und das ist genau das gewünschte ehrliche Signal: "die Menubar sagt mir nichts." Vorher wurde dieser Fall fälschlich als `idle` geraten; jetzt sagt er die Wahrheit.

Dazu kommen noch zwei seltene tmux-Edge-Fälle (eine `pane_dead`- oder `pane_current_path`-Abfrage scheitert auf einer existierenden Session) — praktisch vernachlässigbar, aber sauber als unknown abgefangen statt als Crash.

Der entscheidende Unterschied zu vorher: `unknown` ist jetzt immer ein *bewusst hergeleitetes* Verdikt mit einer dieser konkreten Ursachen — nie mehr "die Funktion ist an einer fehlenden Datei abgestürzt und der Wrapper hat unknown geraten".

Wichtig vorab: ich musste einige Greps wiederholen, weil unsere laufende Session genau über diese Feldnamen redet — `context_management`, `thinking_tokens` etc. tauchen als mein eigener Chat-Text in den Logs auf und verfälschen jede naive Zählung. Die Zahlen unten stammen alle aus **sauberen Fremd-Sessions**.

**Der zentrale Punkt, den man verstehen muss: es gibt drei getrennte Daten-Flächen, nicht eine.** Die Issue tut so, als würde "der Proxy" Header und Response-Usage erfassen — das stimmt so nicht.

**1. Request-Header (die `anthropic-beta`-Flags).** Der Proxy *sieht* sie (`flow.request.headers`), aber er **loggt sie nirgends**. `_build_forwarded_delta` in `logging.py` schreibt Modell, max_tokens, output_config, system/tools/messages — aber kein einziges Header-Feld. In sauberen Dual-Logs: `request_headers`-Feld = 0. Es gibt also aktuell *keine* lesbare Sicht darauf, welche Flag-Untermenge rausgeht. Der 14-Flag-Katalog ist dokumentiert, aber live nicht erfasst. CC schickt je nach Request-Typ unterschiedliche Subsets (6 Core / 13 Main / 9 Structured-Output) — genau diese Verschiebungen wären das Interessante und sind heute unsichtbar.

**2. Request-Body-Direktiven.** Die *werden* geloggt (im `_original` Dual-Log), nur nicht angezeigt. Und hier die erste Korrektur an unserer Doku: CC sendet in praktisch **jedem** Request (117/119, 108/110, …) sowohl `context_management: {edits:[{type: clear_thinking_20251015, keep: all}]}` als auch `diagnostics.previous_message_id`. Unsere OldThemes-Notiz sagt noch "UNVERIFIED ob CC previous_message_id sendet" und "context_management enabled:false" — beides ist jetzt widerlegt: CC fordert aktiv Server-seitiges Thinking-Clearing an und schickt den Companion-Param für Cache-Miss-Diagnose. Die Voraussetzung für die "bedingten" Features ist also erfüllt.

**3. Response-Usage** — und hier liegt die größte Korrektur. Der Proxy reicht erfolgreiche Responses per `stream = True` **ungepuffert durch** (`responseheaders`-Hook), um CCs Token-Streaming nicht zu brechen. Er parst die Response-Usage **gar nicht**. Die einzige Quelle für CR/CC und alles andere ist die **Session-JSONL**, die CC selbst schreibt — und die liest `extract_usage_data` heute aus.

Das Usage-Objekt dort enthält real (über 633 Assistant-Messages aus 8 Sessions konsistent):

- **angezeigt:** `input_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`, `output_tokens` → unser CR/CC/D/Out
- **vorhanden, aber NICHT angezeigt:** `cache_creation` (Split nach `ephemeral_5m` vs `ephemeral_1h` — also welcher Teil in den 1h-Cache ging, direkt am `extended-cache-ttl`-Flag hängend), `server_tool_use` (web_search/web_fetch-Zähler), `service_tier`, `speed`, `iterations` (Per-Iteration-Breakdown, wird mehrelementig wenn Compaction feuert), `inference_geo`
- **Null-Platzhalter, die nur konditional füllen:** `diagnostics` (→ `cache_miss_reason` nur bei echtem Miss) und `context_management` (→ `applied_edits` nur wenn ein Edit feuert)

**Und der dickste Punkt:** `output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens` — von der Issue als "sure win, sitzt im selben Usage-Objekt wie CR/CC" bezeichnet — ist in der Session-JSONL **0 von 633** Messages. CC schreibt es schlicht nicht rein (in *einer* alten Session tauchte es 1× auf, sonst nie). Der vermeintlich einfachste Gewinn ist real der **schwerste**: die verbrauchten Thinking-Tokens bekäme man nur, wenn der Proxy den rohen SSE-Stream puffert und parst — was der aktuelle Stream-Pass-Entwurf bewusst vermeidet.

**Was das für die tatsächliche Rangfolge bedeutet:** Die echten Low-Hanging-Fruits sind die Felder, die *niemand* katalogisiert hat — der 5m/1h-Cache-Split und `server_tool_use` liegen schon im Objekt, das wir parsen, eine triviale Erweiterung in `jsonl_extractors.py`. Die Header-Sichtbarkeit ist mittel (Proxy-Logging muss erst Header erfassen, das ist eine echte `addon.py`-Änderung). Cache-Miss-Reason und applied_edits sind machbar, feuern aber selten. Thinking-Tokens-Consumed ist die einzige, die Proxy-Chirurgie am Response-Stream braucht.

**Was tatsächlich alles zurückfließt** (laut Reference-Doku, jede Response):
- `request-id` — global eindeutige Request-Kennung

- `retry-after` — bei Drosselung die Wartezeit in Sekunden
- `anthropic-priority-*` (nur Priority Tier) und `anthropic-fast-*` (nur Fast Mode)

**Und jetzt der entscheidende mechanische Punkt, der das hier von den thinking_tokens unterscheidet:** Response-**Header** sind nicht Response-**Body**. Das macht sie zum *einfacheren* Gewinn als den vermeintlichen "sure win" der Issue.

📋 PLAN — Step 4: Worker-Scope + Step 5: Deliverables

**Was der Worker macht — reine Write-Seite, beide Header-Quadranten, kein Pane.**

**Quadrant ① Request-Header.** Im `request()`-Hook liegt `flow.request.headers["anthropic-beta"]` zum Zeitpunkt des Forwarded-Writes bereits vor und ist exakt das, was an die API geht (der Proxy fasst den Header nicht an). Der Worker hängt die Flag-Liste als Feld `anthropic_beta` an den `_forwarded`-Eintrag — das ist das "was-an-die-API-geht"-Log und die spätere primäre Lesequelle des Pane-Request-Teils. Voll, nicht delta-codiert (winzig). Kein neues File, keine Janitor-Änderung dafür.

**Quadrant ③ Response-Header.** Hier kommt ein **neues Log** `_response` dazu, gejoint über `flow_id` (genau der Read-Side-Join-Key, den die Architektur dafür vorsieht). Erfasst wird im `responseheaders()`-Hook — dort liegen die Header garantiert vor *bevor* der Body streamt, also ohne den Stream-Pass zu brechen. Pro Response ein Eintrag mit `flow_id`, `request-id` (der kanonische, der zurückkommt), status_code, und der kompletten Rate-Limit-Familie. Die genaue Header-Liste gebe ich dem Worker vor (aus der Reference-Doku, der Worker recherchiert nichts selbst): `request-id`, `retry-after`, `anthropic-organization-id`, und prefix-basiert alles unter `anthropic-ratelimit-*`, `anthropic-priority-*`, `anthropic-fast-*` — prefix-basiert, damit wir robust gegen neue Header sind. Wichtig: **für alle Status-Codes** erfassen, nicht nur 2xx — der `retry-after` erscheint gerade bei 429.

Dazu die Rotation: `response` in die Suffix-Liste in `claude_proxy_start.sh` und einen Registry-Eintrag in `log_janitor.py`, sonst wird das neue Log nie aufgeräumt.

**Explizite Scope-Grenze:** keine Änderung an `parser.py`, `render_turn.py` oder am Pane. Der Worker bringt die Daten nur in die Logs. Read-Seite und Pane-Redesign sind die nächste Phase.

**Drei Files:** `proxy/addon.py` (Header-Erfassung + neues Log-Handle), `core/log_janitor.py` (Registry), `claude_proxy_start.sh` (Rotation). Weil es zwei Hooks und drei Files berührt, läuft der Worker plan-first: erst lesen, Befund + Ansatz melden, STOP — dann Go.

**Deliverables & wie verifiziert wird:**

1. **`anthropic_beta`-Feld im `_forwarded`** — *Ich* verifiziere live nach Proxy-Neustart: frisches `_forwarded` greppen, ob die 6/13/9-Subsets je Request-Typ erscheinen.

You are a WORKER.

Your worktree: `/Users/brunowinter2000/Documents/ai/monitor-cc/.claude/worktrees/header-capture/`
This is your workspace — read, edit, test, and commit here. Do NOT touch files outside this path.

## Context

The mitmproxy addon intercepts every `/v1/messages` request from Claude Code, modifies the body, logs it via a dual-log system, and forwards to Anthropic. Today the proxy logs only request/response **bodies** — it captures **no HTTP headers** in either direction. Two observability gaps exist, both pure WRITE-side (the data is on the wire, we just don't record it):

1. **Request side:** CC sends an `anthropic-beta` HTTP header carrying a set of feature flags (different subsets per request type — roughly 6 / 13 / 9 flags). The proxy passes it through unmodified and discards it. Nothing records which flag-subset went out on a given request.
2. **Response side:** Anthropic returns rate-limit + identity HTTP headers on every response (limit/remaining/reset for requests and tokens, `retry-after` on throttle, the canonical `request-id`). The proxy stream-passes responses and captures none of these.

Goal: record both into the logs. NO display/read-side work in this task (see Negative Scope).

## Investigation targets (read these first)

- `src/proxy/addon.py`:
  - `__init__` (~lines 47-52) — the dual-log file handles, each via `_resolve_dual_log_file("<suffix>")`.
  - `request()` hook — the `_forwarded` write site (~lines 146-157): `_build_forwarded_delta(...)` returns `delta_entry`, then addon sets `delta_entry["flow_id"] = flow.id` and `_write_entry(self.forwarded_log_file, delta_entry)`. Note `flow.request.headers` is already read here (x-request-id at lines 90/131).
  - `responseheaders()` hook (~lines 187-195) — currently only sets `flow.response.stream = True` for 2xx. `flow.response.headers` is fully available here, BEFORE the body streams.
  - `response()` hook (~lines 197-248) — 4xx error logging + `_stripped`/`_injected` writes.
  - `_resolve_dual_log_file(suffix)` (~lines 284-291) — generic over suffix.
- `src/core/log_janitor.py` — `_LOG_REGISTRY` (~lines 25-90): one entry per log for rotation.
- `src/claude_proxy_start.sh` — dual-log rotation; the suffix list is at ~line 189: `for sfx in original forwarded stripped injected errors`.

## Agreed approach (verify it's sound during investigation, flag issues before implementing)

### ① Request beta-flags → field on the existing `_forwarded` log
In `request()`, at the forwarded-write site, before `_write_entry`, add the flag list to the entry:
- read `flow.request.headers.get("anthropic-beta", "")`, split on comma, strip whitespace, drop empties → a list
- store as `delta_entry["anthropic_beta"] = [...]` (full list every entry — it's tiny; do NOT delta-encode)
- This value at this point in `request()` IS what gets forwarded (the proxy does not modify `anthropic-beta`).
- No new file, no janitor change for this part.

### ② Response headers → NEW `_response` dual-log, joined by `flow_id`
- `__init__`: add `self.response_log_file = _resolve_dual_log_file("response")`.
- Capture in `responseheaders()` (headers guaranteed present before stream; does NOT break the stream-pass). Capture for **ALL status codes**, not only 2xx — `retry-after` appears on 429. Do not gate the capture behind the existing `200 <= status < 300` guard (that guard stays for `stream = True`).
- Write one entry per response: `{"flow_id": flow.id, "timestamp": <iso>, "request_id": <from response header>, "status_code": <int>, "headers": {<captured>}}`. Wrap in its own `try/except` like the other dual-log writes — a capture failure must never affect forwarding.
- **Which headers to capture** (this is the Anthropic API contract — do NOT research it, use exactly this; mitmproxy header names are case-insensitive, normalize to lowercase): capture any header whose lowercased name is one of `request-id`, `retry-after`, `anthropic-organization-id`, OR starts with one of the prefixes `anthropic-ratelimit-`, `anthropic-priority-`, `anthropic-fast-`. Prefix-based so we stay robust to new headers in those families. The concrete rate-limit set is `anthropic-ratelimit-{requests,tokens,input-tokens,output-tokens}-{limit,remaining,reset}`.

### Rotation (so the new log gets cleaned up)
- `claude_proxy_start.sh` line ~189: add `response` to the suffix list.
- `core/log_janitor.py` `_LOG_REGISTRY`: add an entry for `dual_log/api_requests_*_response.jsonl` mirroring the existing dual-log entries (writer `proxy/addon.py:_resolve_dual_log_file`, purpose: "Dual-log: response HTTP headers (rate-limit family + request-id), joined by flow_id", same retention/janitor_trigger as the other dual-log suffixes).

## Negative Scope (do NOT do)
- Do NOT touch the read side: no changes to `proxy_display/parser.py`, `render_turn.py`, `pane.py`, or any pane/display module.
- Do NOT design or implement any UI/pane rendering of the new data.
- Do NOT modify request `anthropic-beta` (it stays pass-through) or any other header.
- Do NOT add fields/features beyond the two captures + rotation above.

## Verification (you cannot run the live proxy from the worktree)
The running proxy uses a frozen copy and needs real CC traffic — you can't test live. Instead:
- Write a lightweight smoke that exercises the header-capture logic with a mock object mimicking the mitmproxy flow (`.request.headers`, `.response.headers`, `.id`, `.metadata`, `.response.status_code`). Assert: `anthropic_beta` list is built correctly from a comma-joined header; the `_response` entry contains the expected filtered headers + flow_id + request_id for 2xx AND for a 429 with `retry-after`. If extracting the filter into a small testable helper makes this clean, do so.
- Run a python import/syntax smoke on the changed modules.
- The LIVE verification (real headers in real logs after proxy restart) is done by Opus after merge — not your responsibility.

- Files changed: [list]
- ① `anthropic_beta` capture: where added, exact field shape

- Rotation: registry entry + suffix-list edit confirmed

## Origin

Emerged from the `anthropic-beta` flag research (see `proxy_header_mods.md`). Conclusion there: no flag is worth stripping — the proxy's value is observation, not mutation. The constructive inverse of stripping: surface the request/response data on the wire in the monitor pane.

## Status

- **Write-side header capture: DONE** (this session, merged on dev). `_forwarded` now carries `anthropic_beta` (full flag list per request); a new `_response` dual-log captures the response HTTP-header rate-limit family + `request-id` in the `responseheaders()` hook for all status codes. See `decisions/logging.md`, `decisions/pipe05_proxy_cache.md`, `src/proxy/DOCS.md`, smoke `dev/hook_smoke/test_header_capture.py`.
- **Read-side + pane redesign: PENDING** (next session). Surface the captured + already-logged-but-unshown fields; restructure the proxy pane along Request/Response. Tracked in the umbrella issue.
- **Live-verify: PENDING** a proxy restart (frozen live-copy) — grep fresh `_forwarded` for 6/13/9 beta subsets, grep `_response` for real `anthropic-ratelimit-*` (confirms the headers survive on our OAuth traffic).

## Data anatomy — four quadrants (Request/Response × Header/Body)

The monitor confuses two axes. An API exchange splits into direction (Request out / Response back) × layer (HTTP header / body). Today's pane covers ~one quadrant.

| Quadrant | Content | Captured where (WRITE) | Shown (READ) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ① Request-Header | `anthropic-beta` flags (6/13/9 subsets), auth | NOW `_forwarded.anthropic_beta` (was: nowhere) | ❌ not yet |
| ② Request-Body | model, max_tokens, output_config, thinking, system, tools, messages, context_management, diagnostics | `_original` (full), `_forwarded` (delta) | mostly ✅ (sys/tools/messages/fields); context_management + diagnostics ❌ |
| ③ Response-Header | rate-limit family, request-id, retry-after, org-id | NOW `_response` (was: nowhere) | ❌ not yet |
| ④ Response-Body | usage object + content blocks | Session-JSONL (CC writes; proxy stream-passes, captures none) | content ✅, CR/CC/D/Out ✅; rest ❌ |

The screenshot-era pane header row already mixes axes: `eff:`/`think:` are ② (request budget we ask for), `CR:`/`CC:` are ④ (response, joined from Session-JSONL).

## Two log systems (do not confuse)

- **Proxy dual-logs** (`src/logs/dual_log/`, proxy-written): request side (`_original`/`_forwarded`/`_stripped`/`_injected`), `_errors` (4xx), and now `_response` (resp headers). Proxy does NOT capture the response BODY — 2xx is `stream = True` pass-through.
- **Session-JSONL** (`~/.claude/projects/**/*.jsonl`, CC-written): the response `usage` object. Sole source of CR/CC/D/Out and all other usage sub-fields.

## Candidate data — corrected by this session's evidence

### Response-Body usage fields (Session-JSONL) — the real low-hanging fruit
Full `usage` object observed (633 assistant msgs / 8 sessions, consistent): `input_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`, `output_tokens` (shown) + **not shown:** `cache_creation` (`ephemeral_5m`/`ephemeral_1h` split — ties to `extended-cache-ttl`), `server_tool_use` (`web_search`/`web_fetch` counts), `service_tier`, `speed`, `iterations` (per-iteration breakdown; multi-element on compaction), `inference_geo`. These are already in the object `extract_usage_data` (jsonl_extractors.py) parses → trivial read-side extension. **Highest value-per-effort.**

### thinking_tokens — SUPERSEDED ("sure win" was wrong)
Earlier claim: `usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens` sits in the same usage object → trivial. **Falsified:** `output_tokens_details` is present **0 / 633** assistant msgs across 8 recent sessions (appeared once in one old session). CC does NOT persist it to Session-JSONL. The consumed-reasoning value lives only in the raw SSE response stream, which the proxy stream-passes without buffering. → NOT trivial; the **hardest** candidate (needs proxy SSE buffering, breaks the stream-pass design). Today's `think:Nk` = requested budget (max_tokens), not consumed.

### cache_miss_reason — companion param VERIFIED (was "UNVERIFIED")
`diagnostics.cache_miss_reason` (from `cache-diagnosis` flag) needs CC to send `diagnostics.previous_message_id`. **Verified CC sends it on ~every request** (117/119, 108/110, 40/41 in clean `_original` logs). Response-side `diagnostics` key is present but `null` normally — `cache_miss_reason` populates only on actual cache-miss events (conditional/rare-firing), readable from Session-JSONL.

### applied_edits — feature ACTIVE (was "enabled:false")
`context_management.applied_edits` (from `context-management` flag). **Verified CC sends `context_management: {edits:[{type:clear_thinking_20251015, keep:all}]}` on ~every request** (same logs). Response-side `context_management` key present but `null` normally; `applied_edits` populates only when an edit fires (conditional). Readable from Session-JSONL.

### Request-Body directives (②, already logged, not shown)
`context_management` + `diagnostics.previous_message_id` sit in `_original`/`_forwarded` today — pure read-side gap.

## Response-Header rate-limit family (③, NEW candidate)

Anthropic returns on every response (reference: `monitor-cc-reference: platform_claude_com_docs_en_api_rate_limits.md`, `api_overview.md`): `request-id`, `anthropic-organization-id`, and the rate-limit family `anthropic-ratelimit-{requests,tokens,input-tokens,output-tokens}-{limit,remaining,reset}` (reset = RFC-3339), `retry-after` (on throttle), `anthropic-priority-*` (Priority Tier), `anthropic-fast-*` (fast mode). This is "how close to the throttle / when it resets" — observability the monitor has zero of, complementing the consumption view (CR/CC/Out).

**Key mechanic:** response HEADERS arrive with the status line *before* the body streams → readable in `responseheaders()` for free, no body buffering, no stream-pass break. This is why ③ is an EASY win and thinking_tokens (response BODY) is hard.

## Beta-header provenance (why we "knew" about 14 flags)

The 14-flag catalog (`proxy_header_mods.md`) was extracted from an **old top-level `api_requests_*.jsonl`** that had a `request_headers` field. That format was replaced by the dual-log system (bodies only), the header-reading code was removed with the beta-manipulation block, and the source files rotated away (cited file is gone). → the catalog was a frozen snapshot from a deleted log; no live capture existed until this session's `_forwarded.anthropic_beta`.

## Prioritization (post-correction)

1. **Read-side, Session-JSONL extras** (cache_creation split, server_tool_use, service_tier/speed, iterations, inference_geo) — data already parsed-adjacent, highest value-per-effort.
2. **Read-side, response headers** (`_response` now written) — surface rate-limit family in the new pane RESPONSE block.
3. **Read-side, request beta-flags** (`_forwarded.anthropic_beta` now written) + request directives — pane REQUEST block.
4. **Conditional** cache_miss_reason / applied_edits — wire up, rare-firing.
5. **Hard** thinking_tokens consumed — only via proxy SSE buffering; defer.

## Pane redesign direction

Proxy pane = visual single-source-of-truth (logs are for Opus). Restructure per Request/Response: a REQUEST block (sys/tools/messages/fields + beta-flag subset indicator) and a RESPONSE block (usage extras + rate-limit headers). Two-block cut preferred over bolting a response section onto the request-centric layout — "request solid, response absent" is the imbalance to fix, not cement.

## Open

- Live-verify (proxy restart) of `_forwarded.anthropic_beta` subsets + `_response` rate-limit headers on OAuth traffic.
- Read-side extraction shapes + pane-redesign layout (next session).

## Scope note

`src/` changes (capture done; read-side + pane rendering pending) are worker tasks. This file is the chat-derived concept + evidence trail.

- Empirical: clean `_original` dual-logs (context_management/previous_message_id presence), 8 Session-JSONLs / 633 assistant msgs (usage object shape, output_tokens_details absence).
- `decisions/OldThemes/proxy_header_mods.md` (14-flag catalogue + provenance).

"cache_read": 78469, "content_blocks": [{"chars": 0, "output_tokens": 5394, "sig_chars": 14136, "type": "thinking"}, {"preview": "Es ist effektiv **eine** Domain — `platform.claude.com/docs/en/`. Da liegt das ganze Fleisch, kanonisch und versioniert. Die `anthropic.com/news`- und `/engineering`-Seiten sind nur Blog-Announcements, die Specs stehen alle unter `platform.claude.com`. Innerhalb dieser Domain sind aber nur bestimmte Sektions-Bäume für unsere zwei Issues relevant — der Rest (Vision, PDF, Embeddings, Citations, Prompt-Engineering-Guides, Tutorials, die Cloud-Plattform-Seiten für Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry, Agent-SDK, Computer-Use, MCP) ist Ballast und kommt beim Cull raus.

**Relevant für #7 (Modell-Limits + over-limit-Verhalten):**

- `about-claude/models/overview` — die Max-output-Tabelle, aus der ich gerade die 64k/128k-Ceilings habe. Kernquelle.
- `about-claude/models/` Rest — pricing, model-ids-and-versions, migration-guide. Liefert die Modell-IDs und Versionslogik, die wir im Override referenzieren.
- `api/models/list` — die Models-API, die `max_tokens`/`max_input_tokens` pro Modell programmatisch zurückgibt. Das ist die maschinenlesbare Variante des Ceilings, falls wir den Override per-Modell dynamisch bauen wollen.
- `api/errors` — das Validierungsverhalten (400 vs. clamp vs. default). Die offene #7-Frage.
- `api/messages` + `api/rate-limits` — die `max_tokens`-Parameter-Spec und Token-Durchsatzlimits.

**Relevant für #3 (die 13 Beta-Flags):**

Hier ist die Zuordnung Flag → Doc-Seite das eigentliche Steuerrad für den Cull. Die meisten Flags hängen an einer konkreten Feature-Seite unter `build-with-claude/`:

| Flag | Doc-Seite |
|---|---|
| `interleaved-thinking`, `redact-thinking`, `thinking-token-count` | `build-with-claude/extended-thinking` |
| `context-1m` | `build-with-claude/context-windows` |
| `context-management` | `build-with-claude/context-management` (context-editing) |
| `prompt-caching-scope`, `extended-cache-ttl`, `cache-diagnosis` | `build-with-claude/prompt-caching` |
| `effort` | `build-with-claude/effort` |
| `advanced-tool-use`, `advisor-tool` | `agents-and-tools/tool-use` + `advanced-tool-use` |
| alle 13 (Datum→Feature-Mapping) | `release-notes/overview` + `api/beta-headers` |

Die `release-notes/overview` ist dabei besonders wertvoll: Release-Notes mappen `feature-name-YYYY-MM-DD` direkt auf das, was an dem Datum eingeführt wurde — genau das brauchen wir für die Datums-Flags. Zwei Flags — `claude-code-20250219` und `oauth-2025-04-20` — sind vermutlich interne CC-Flags, die gar nicht in der öffentlichen API-Doc stehen. Das werden wir nach dem Index sehen; wenn RAG dazu nichts findet, ist das selbst das Ergebnis ("undokumentiert, intern").

Die entscheidende Stelle ist die `stop_reason`-Doku (in `messages/create` und `messages/batches/results`):

Die API kennt also „the model's maximum" als eigenständige, server-seitig durchgesetzte Grenze. Zusammen mit unserer empirischen Tatsache (Sonnet-Worker laufen mit `max_tokens=128000` → **kein 400**) ergibt das die Antwort auf deine Frage:

**Die API clampt auf das Modell-Ceiling. Sie fällt nicht auf einen niedrigen Default zurück.** Konkret heißt das:
- **Sonnet** `128000` → effektiv **64k** (das Ceiling), Generation stoppt bei 64k mit `stop_reason="max_tokens"`.
- **Opus** `128000` → exakt **128k**, gültig.

Damit ist die ursprüngliche #7-Befürchtung — „pinnt die Anfrage auf einen 32k-Default = Downgrade gegenüber dem, was CC original schickt" — **widerlegt**. Es ist kein Downgrade, es ist ein Clamp nach oben gedeckelt auf 64k, was über CC's Default liegt.

**Ehrlichkeits-Split:** Ceilings (64k/128k), das `minimum:0`-Schema und die `stop_reason`-Semantik sind dokumentierte Fakten; das „kein 400" ist empirisch aus unserem System. Das Wort „clamp" selbst steht nicht explizit da — es ist die zwingende Schlussfolgerung aus „stop_reason = requested max_tokens *oder* the model's maximum" plus dem fehlenden 400. Wenn du es als harten Fakt statt als (sehr starke) Inferenz willst: die **Models-API** `GET /v1/models/{id}` gibt `max_tokens` pro Modell authoritativ zurück — ein Call, dann ist auch das Ceiling maschinell bestätigt.

## Effort: ist `high` das Sonnet-Maximum?

**Nein.** Es gibt fünf Stufen — `low < medium < high < xhigh < max` — aber sie sind nicht alle auf jedem Modell verfügbar:

- **`max`** ist auf Sonnet 4.6 verfügbar (explizit gelistet: "Available on … and Claude Sonnet 4.6").
- **`xhigh`** ist **nur auf Opus 4.8 und Opus 4.7** — *nicht* auf Sonnet.

Für Sonnet 4.6 ist die Leiter also `low / medium / high / max`, und das Maximum ist **`max`**, nicht `high`. Wenn du Sonnet wirklich ans Limit fahren willst, ist `max` der richtige Wert; `xhigh` würde auf Sonnet nicht greifen (Opus-only).

Zwei Nuancen, die direkt unsere Config betreffen: Erstens sagt die Doc ausdrücklich, **`high` = „exactly the same behavior as omitting the effort parameter"** — es ist der Default. Unser Worker-Override `effort: high` pinnt also nur den Default und bewirkt für sich genommen nichts. Zweitens ist Anthropics *empfohlener* Sonnet-Default sogar `medium` ("best balance"), mit der Warnung, effort bei Sonnet explizit zu setzen. Heißt konkret: willst du Worker stärker → `max`; willst du sie billiger/schneller → `medium`; `high` ist die Mitte und aktuell redundant zum Default. (Der Opus-Block mit `xhigh` ist dagegen sauber — gültig für Opus 4.8.) Soll ich den Worker-Effort anpassen, oder lassen wir `high`?

## Header 1 — `interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14`

**Was es macht:** Aktiviert *interleaved thinking* — Claude denkt **zwischen** Tool-Calls: es kann über ein Tool-Ergebnis nachdenken bevor es den nächsten Schritt entscheidet, und mehrere Tool-Calls mit Reasoning-Schritten dazwischen verketten. Nur für Tools über die Messages API relevant.

**Auf unseren Modellen ist es ein No-op.** Die entscheidende Stelle:

> "The Claude API accepts `interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14` in requests to any model without returning an error. On models that don't support interleaved thinking, the header is ignored. On Claude Opus 4.8, 4.7, and 4.6 it's **deprecated and safely ignored**."

Wir fahren Opus 4.8 (main) und Sonnet 4.6 (worker), beide mit `thinking: adaptive`. Auf beiden ist interleaved thinking bei adaptive thinking **automatisch an — ganz ohne Header**. Der Header adressiert den alten *manuellen* Modus (`thinking: {type:"enabled"}`) und ist auf Opus 4.8 explizit deprecated+ignored, auf Sonnet 4.6 "still functional but deprecated". Da wir adaptive nutzen, läuft er ins Leere.

**Verdikt:** harmlos, wirkungslos auf unserem Setup. CC schickt ihn nur für Rückwärtskompatibilität mit älteren Modellen (Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.1 etc.), wo er den manuellen Modus erst freischaltet. **Proxy-Konsequenz: durchreichen, nichts manipulieren** — ein Strip würde auf unseren Modellen exakt nichts ändern, weil er ohnehin ignoriert wird.

Nächstes im Thinking-Trio wäre `redact-thinking-2026-02-12`. ## Header 2 — `redact-thinking-2026-02-12`

**Der Flag-Name steht nicht im Korpus** — kein einziger wörtlicher Treffer. Das **Feature** dahinter ist aber dokumentiert: die API kann `redacted_thinking`-Blocks zurückgeben — verschlüsselter Thinking-Inhalt in einem opaken `data`-Feld, der entsteht, wenn Teile von Claudes Reasoning **sicherheits-redigiert** werden. Das ist ein eigener Block-Typ, verschieden von normalen Thinking-Blocks und von `display:"omitted"`. Beim Multi-Turn-Tool-Roundtrip müssen diese Blocks **unverändert zurückgeschickt** werden, sonst bricht das Protokoll.

**Ehrlichkeits-Split:** Dass `redact-thinking-2026-02-12` genau dieses Feature steuert, ist plausible Inferenz (Name + Datum passen), **kein dokumentierter Fakt** — die Anthropic-Doc bindet den Flag-Namen nirgends an das Feature.

**Verdikt:** durchreichen, **auf keinen Fall strippen**. Doppelter Grund: erstens kennen wir die exakte Wirkung nicht (undokumentiert), zweitens ist das Redacted-Thinking-Roundtrip korrektheits-kritisch — wenn der Flag dem Server signalisiert „Client kann redacted_thinking-Blocks verarbeiten", könnte ein Strip die Response-Shape oder das Fehlerverhalten ändern. Das ist die konservativste und sicherste Linie.

DELIVERABLE 2 — CREATE `decisions/OldThemes/model_override_limits.md`
============================================================
The investigation trail for the model-override max_tokens + effort question. Structure as process documentation (concern: what was the worry, what the research found, what was decided). Content:

**Original concern:** proxy `_inject_model_override` (src/proxy/inject_helpers.py, values from `~/.claude/shared-rules/proxy_rules.json`) rewrote BOTH the main (opus) and worker (sonnet) requests to `max_tokens: 128000`. Worry: if a model's documented ceiling is below 128k, does the API hard-400, silently clamp, or silently fall back to a low default (a DOWNGRADE vs what CC originally sent)? And: is `effort: high` the maximum settable for Sonnet?

**Research (source: `monitor-cc-reference` Anthropic docs):**
- Per-model max output (synchronous Messages API): Opus 4.8 = 128k, Sonnet 4.6 = 64k, Haiku 4.5 = 64k. Source: `about_claude_models_overview.md` (Max output row) + `extended_thinking.md`. The 300k figure is Batches-API-only via `output-300k-2026-03-24`.
- `max_tokens` param schema: `minimum 0`, NO maximum enforced in schema; "Different models have different maximum values for this parameter." Source: `api_messages_create.md`.
- `stop_reason: "max_tokens"` is documented as "we exceeded the requested `max_tokens` **or the model's maximum**". Source: `api_messages_create.md` + `api_messages_batches_results.md`.
- Over-limit behavior conclusion: API CLAMPS to the model ceiling. Evidence chain: (1) schema enforces no max → no schema-400; (2) empirical — our Sonnet workers run with max_tokens=128000 and get no 400; (3) stop_reason references "the model's maximum" as a real server-side cap. Therefore Sonnet's 128000 is effectively 64k (clamped), NOT pinned to a low default. The original "silent downgrade" fear is REFUTED. NOTE honestly: "clamp" is the necessary inference from stop_reason wording + no-400; the docs do not contain a literal "we clamp max_tokens" statement. Authoritative confirmation available via Models API `GET /v1/models/{id}.max_tokens` (not yet queried).
- Effort: 5 levels `low < medium < high < xhigh < max` (`effort.md` Effort-levels table). `max` available on Sonnet 4.6; `xhigh` ONLY on Opus 4.8 / Opus 4.7 (not Sonnet). `high` = "exactly the same behavior as omitting the effort parameter" (the default). Sonnet's recommended default is `medium`; its ceiling is `max`. So `high` is NOT Sonnet's max — `max` is. The effort PARAMETER needs no beta header ("available on all supported models with no beta header required").

**Decisions:**
- Worker (sonnet) `max_tokens`: 128000 → **64000** (Sonnet 4.6 real ceiling). Applied this session in `~/.claude/shared-rules/proxy_rules.json` `model_override_worker`. Rationale: 128000 was harmless (clamped to 64k) but dishonest — config + proxy-pane displayed 128k that could never apply.
- Opus `max_tokens`: keep **128000** (= Opus 4.8 ceiling, exact).
- Worker (sonnet) `effort`: keep **high**. Rationale: workers also run investigations; high (the solid default) is preferred over medium. `max` is available but not chosen. NOTE: do NOT set `xhigh` on the worker — xhigh is Opus-only on Sonnet it does not apply.
- Opus `effort`: keep **xhigh** (valid for Opus 4.8).

**Open:** Models-API confirmation of the clamp behavior (one `GET /v1/models/{id}` call) not yet done.

Logik: Wir sind ein Monitor — unser Wert ist Beobachten. Statt Header zu *entfernen* (bringt nichts), lesen wir die **Antwort-Daten**, die einige dieser Flags erzeugen, und zeigen sie in der Pane an. Die Flags werden so von „CC verhandelt was mit der API" zu „Observability, die wir sichtbar machen".

Konkret drei Felder aus der Response:

1. **`usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens`** — wie viele der abgerechneten Output-Tokens internes Reasoning waren. Das ist der sichere Treffer: GA, ohne Header, in *jeder* Response. Damit könntest du pro Request sehen, welcher Anteil des Outputs Thinking war vs. echte Antwort. Die Pane zeigt heute `think:64k` (das ist das *Budget*, das wir setzen) — hier ginge es um das tatsächlich *verbrauchte* Thinking.

2. **`diagnostics.cache_miss_reason`** (aus `cache-diagnosis`) — bei einem Cache-Miss sagt dir das Feld *wo* der Prefix divergiert ist: Model, System, Tools oder Messages. Das ist für ein Cache-Monitoring-Tool Gold — statt nur zu sehen, dass CC auf 0 fällt (heute die CC-Spalte), sähest du den *Grund*. **Aber:** erscheint nur, wenn CC zusätzlich den Body-Parameter `diagnostics.previous_message_id` mitschickt. Header allein reicht nicht — und ob CC den Param sendet, ist ungeprüft (steht in den Request-Bodies, ein Log-Check entfernt).

3. **`context_management.applied_edits`** — wenn server-seitiges Context-Editing feuert, meldet die Response, was geleert wurde (wie viele Tool-Uses, wie viele Tokens). Nur aktiv, wenn das Feature läuft — aktuell ist unser Proxy-`context_management` aber `enabled: false`.

Dein CoinDesk-Beispiel macht den Punkt glasklar: der GitHub-Reflex war der falsche Default, die autoritative Quelle für „hat CoinDesk eine Sitemap" ist CoinDesk's robots.txt selbst.

Und schöne Ironie: Das OldThemes hier ist chat-derived — nach der Regel, die wir gerade geschrieben haben, schreibe **ich** das direkt, kein Worker. Erste Anwendung der neuen Regel. Ich fange damit an.", "type": "text"}, {"preview": {"content": "# Proxy Read-Side Observability

## Origin

Emerged from the `anthropic-beta` flag research (see `proxy_header_mods.md`). Conclusion there: no flag is worth stripping — the proxy's value is observation, not mutation. The constructive inverse of stripping: surface the response-side / header data these flags produce in the monitor pane.

## Concept

The proxy already reads response usage — the pane's `CR` / `CC` values are `cache_read_input_tokens` / `cache_creation_input_tokens` from the response `usage` object. Read-side observability = extend that existing capture to surface MORE of what is already on the wire, instead of mutating requests.

## Candidate data

### 1. thinking_tokens — sure win

`usage.output_tokens_details.thinking_tokens` — billed output tokens spent on internal reasoning.

```json
"usage": { "output_tokens": 348, "output_tokens_details": { "thinking_tokens": 312 } }
```

Always present (GA, no header — historically gated by `thinking-token-count-2026-05-13`, now headerless). Sits in the SAME `usage` object the pane already reads for CR/CC → trivial extension. Pane today shows `think:Nk` = the BUDGET we set (`max_tokens`/effort), not the CONSUMED reasoning; `thinking_tokens` is the consumed value.

### 2. cache_miss_reason — conditional

From `cache-diagnosis-2026-04-07`. On a cache miss, `diagnostics.cache_miss_reason` reports WHERE the prompt prefix diverged (model / system / tools / messages). Turns the pane's "CC dropped to 0" into "WHY it dropped". CONDITION: appears only if CC also sends the body param `diagnostics.previous_message_id` — the header is necessary-but-not-sufficient. UNVERIFIED whether CC sends it.

### 3. applied_edits — conditional

From `context-management-2025-06-27`. Response `context_management.applied_edits` reports what server-side context-editing cleared (tool_uses, tokens). CONDITION: only when the feature is active — the proxy's own `context_management` is currently `enabled: false`.

## Header visibility gap

The `anthropic-beta` flags CC sends live ONLY in the `request_headers` field of the top-level `api_requests_*.jsonl`. They are NOT surfaced in the pane and NOT in `dual_log/` (body-deltas only). Right now there is no live readable view of which flags go out. A pane element showing the per-request flag set would make subset-shifts visible — e.g. a request carrying only the 6-flag universal core vs the full 13 (subsets documented in `proxy_header_mods.md`).

## Open

- Verify in the request bodies whether CC sends the companion params `diagnostics.previous_message_id` (cache-diagnosis) and `context_management` (decides whether #2 / #3 are achievable at all).
- Locate the current response-usage capture path: the top-level `api_requests_*.jsonl` rotates and was absent when this was written; `dual_log/` carries request-body deltas only. Confirm where the pane reads CR/CC from so `thinking_tokens` can hook the same path.

## Scope note

`src/` changes (pane rendering + response-usage extension) are a worker task. This file is the chat-derived concept trail.

Zuerst die Counter-Check-Sektion → „External Resource Assessment" (assess *ob* extern nötig, dann *was* und *welche Quelle* — keine GitHub-Default-Reflexe).", "type": "text"}, {"preview": {"file_path": "/Users/brunowinter2000/.claude/shared-rules/opus/workers-1.md", "new_string": "### External Resource Assessment

At key investigation moments the discipline is to STOP grinding hypotheses in-head and ask two questions IN ORDER:

1. **Do I need external information at all?** Often no — `<Project>-docs` RAG plus the project's own source code answer it. If the question is fully answerable from indexed docs + code, do NOT reach for any external tool.
2. **If yes — what EXACT fact/artifact do I need, and which source is authoritative FOR THAT question?**

The **source decides, not a default tool.** Match the question to where the answer actually lives:

- **The system/source itself** — authoritative for its own config and behavior. "Does site X have a sitemap, how deep?" → fetch `X/robots.txt` + the sitemap index directly. "What does API Y return for field Z?" → the vendor's API reference. First choice whenever the question is about a specific system's own behavior.
- **Vendor / framework docs** — API semantics, library behavior, config options.
- **General web** — behavioral / how-to questions, error-message lookups.
- **GitHub / code search** — "how do people actually implement X", real shipped patterns, reading a dependency's source.
- **Reddit / forums** — experiential reports and gotchas ("X blocks long scraping sessions", undocumented quirks).
- **Papers (arxiv)** — methodology, algorithms, academic grounding.

**No reflexive default.** Routing every external question to GitHub is a known failure mode: GitHub shows what OTHER people assumed (often stale) when the authoritative answer is one direct fetch from the source. Before reaching for ANY search tool, name the specific resource you need and justify the source.

**Trigger moments to run this assessment** — these are when in-head grinding is most costly:
1. Entering PLAN Step 2 on a new feature touching platform / framework / library surface.
2. After a failed iteration — "approach refuted" / "still doesn't work" / about to form hypothesis N+1.
3. A bug recurs after a RAG-aided fix attempt (second iteration on the same bug).

At each trigger: pause, run question (1) then (2). When external info IS needed, state — to the user, or in the PLAN — WHAT you need and WHERE you will get it BEFORE fetching, especially when more than one source could plausibly serve. Opus is the sole fetcher of external knowledge (§ External Knowledge — Opus Provides, Worker Implements); the worker never searches externally.", "old_string": "### GitHub-Search Counter-Check (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

External-pattern verification via `gh-cli-search` skill fires at TWO trigger points:

1. **BEFORE dispatching any worker on a new feature / new architectural problem** — when the task touches platform APIs, framework conventions, library integrations, or any "how do people normally do X" surface. Cost: 1–3 `gh-cli search_code` / `search_repos` calls (~30 seconds). Gain: convergent patterns from real shipped code that prevent hypothesis-grinding.
2. **AFTER any failed iteration on a hard problem** — when one investigation cycle ended with "approach refuted" / "still doesn't work" / "let's try another hypothesis". BEFORE the next hypothesis is formed in-head, run gh-search. If two independent repos converge on the same pattern, that pattern wins over any in-head hypothesis.
3. **BUG RECURS AFTER RAG-AIDED FIX ATTEMPT** — bug → RAG hint → fix → bug still there. Second iteration on the same bug. Before another hypothesis: gh-search the symptom + API surface. No more in-head trial-and-error past iteration 2.

**Hard rule:** when entering PLAN Step 2 (Prep Investigation) on a new feature touching platform/framework/library surface OR when about to formulate a new hypothesis after a failed iteration → activate `gh-cli-search` skill (search strategy is in the skill itself), then proceed with the PLAN.

/latest-crypto-news ist CoinDesks chronologische "Neueste-Artikel"-Übersichtsseite — die Feed-Seite, auf der alle Artikel newest-first gelistet sind, quer über alle Sektionen (Markets, Tech, Policy etc.). Sie lädt
   zunächst ~16 Artikel und blendet per "More stories"-Button immer ältere nach (Infinite-Scroll-Prinzip). Genau da klinkt sich unser Discover ein: Seite öffnen, alle Artikel-Links mit /YYYY/MM/DD/-Datum einsammeln,
   "More stories" klicken um weiter zurückzugehen. Es ist also das Eingangstor zur Chronologie — und genau deshalb die naheliegende Backfill-Quelle: theoretisch kommst du über genug Klicks beliebig weit zurück, wenn
   das Feed das hergibt.

  Scope-Verschiebung ist logisch, ja. Diese Session: nur CoinDesk, messen wie weit zurück wir kommen. Tägliche Pipe, Cron, Logging, Multi-Site — alles vertagt. Pro Seite ein eigener Backfill, und "je weiter zurück
  desto besser" stimmt — wobei der Wert der Tiefe konkret aus dem Backtesting kommt: für Live-Trading auf 1s-Candles ist 3 Jahre alte News wertlos, aber um den Algo gegen historische Candles zu backtesten brauchst
  du News, die zeitlich zu genau diesen Candles passen. Deshalb: so tief wie geht.

  Bevor ich den Probe scope, hole ich die externe Info die das Probe-Design entscheidet — nämlich ob CoinDesk neben dem Feed eine Sitemap mit historischer Tiefe hat (Arc Publishing). Das bestimmt, ob wir per
  Browser-Pagination klicken müssen oder einfach XML parsen können. Ich such das auf GitHub bei echten Scrapern ab.

⏺ Skill(gh-cli-search)

❯ warte mal du willst auf gh suchen ob coin desk eine sitemap hat? welche information bruachst du von extern, bitte nenne, und sage mir wo du am liebsten suchen würdest. Im generellen web, auf reddit oder gh.

⏺ Berechtigter Stopp — mein GitHub-Reflex war hier der falsche Default. Lass mich sauber auseinanderziehen, was ich überhaupt brauche und woher es am besten kommt.

  Ich brauche genau zwei externe Infos:

  1. Welche Sitemaps publiziert CoinDesk und wie weit reichen sie zurück? Das ist die entscheidende Frage — sie bestimmt, ob wir den Backfill sauber per XML-Parsing machen können (vollständig, kein Browser, schnell)
   oder ob wir uns durch das Feed klicken müssen. CoinDesk läuft auf Arc Publishing; die Google-News-Sitemap, die wir früher hatten, deckt nur ~48h ab. Ob es daneben eine vollständige Archiv-Sitemap mit
  Monaten/Jahren Tiefe gibt, weiß ich nicht.

  2. (sekundär) Wie aggressiv blockt CoinDesk lange Scraping-Sessions? Relevant nur, falls wir die Feed-Pagination-Route gehen müssen — eine 500-Klick-Session läuft anders Gefahr geblockt zu werden als ein einzelner
   Sitemap-Fetch.

  Wo ich suchen würde — und das ist der Punkt: Für Frage 1 ist die beste Quelle CoinDesk selbst, nicht GitHub oder Reddit. Ein direkter Fetch von coindesk.com/robots.txt listet normalerweise alle Sitemap-Locations
  auf, und der Sitemap-Index sagt mir in Sekunden, wie tief die URLs zurückreichen. Das ist autoritativ und aktuell. GitHub/Reddit wären hier zweite Wahl — die zeigen mir nur, was andere Scraper angenommen haben,
  und das ist potenziell veraltet (CoinDesk hat neu designt, unsere eigenen iter-3/4-Notizen zeigen schon, dass die alte Sitemap-Route gekappt/aufgegeben wurde).

  GitHub wird erst interessant, wenn die Sitemap-Route tot ist — dann lohnt es zu sehen, wie andere tiefe CoinDesk-Historie geholt haben oder welche Anti-Bot-Tricks sie für lange Pagination nutzen. Reddit eher für
  "CoinDesk blockt mich"-Erfahrungsberichte, also Frage 2.

  Eine Sache kann keine externe Quelle beantworten: wie tief das /latest-crypto-news-Feed selbst paginiert, bevor es serverseitig dichtmacht. **Was das Grün bedeutet und was kaputt ist**

Das Grün in der Proxy-Pane heißt „diesen Inhalt hat der Proxy injiziert" (gelb = gestrippt, grün = eingefügt). Der Bug: unveränderter CC-Inhalt wird grün markiert, obwohl der Proxy ihn nie angefasst hat. In Bild 4 ist es genau das — der `<persisted-output>`-Block ist CC's eigener Output, byte-identisch in original und forwarded, trotzdem grün.

**Die Ursache, wie sie dokumentiert ist**

Ursprünglich kam die Färbung aus einem Text-Diff (`_diff_text` in `diff_engine.py`), der zwei Dinge falsch machte. Erstens lief er auf dem JSON-serialisierten Block (`{"tool_use_id":…, "content":…}`), sodass Färbung in JSON-Strukturzeichen blutete. Zweitens, und das ist der Kern: wenn direkt neben einem gestrippten Stück (z.B. einer entfernten `<system-reminder>`) unveränderter Inhalt steht, der einen gemeinsamen Prefix teilt, dann markiert der Diff diesen Prefix fälschlich als gleichzeitig gestrippt UND injiziert — daher das Phantom-Grün auf unverändertem Text.

**Der Fix, der schon drin ist**

Statt zu diffen wird jetzt aus den Aufzeichnungen gebaut: der Proxy injiziert auf Messages nie, er strippt nur. `apply_modification_rules` protokolliert exakt, was es pro Message gestrippt hat (`stripped_msg_removed`). `build_message_spans` nimmt diese Aufzeichnung, markiert die gestrippten Stücke gelb und alles, was im forwarded-Text echt neu dazukommt, grün. Das ist auf vier Fällen mit null Phantom validiert.

**Wo ich glaube, dass es weiterhin bricht (Hypothese, nicht bewiesen)**

Dieser Fix hat ein bewusstes Gate: er läuft nur, wenn der Block einen aufgezeichneten Strip-Chunk hat, der sich im Block-Text wiederfindet (`blk_chunks` nicht leer). Blöcke, die das nicht erfüllen, fallen auf den alten, kaputten `_diff_text`-Pfad zurück. Dann kommt das ursprüngliche Phantom-Grün dort zurück. Der GT-Fix hat das Symptom also für den Hauptfall behoben, aber das Loch im Fallback offen gelassen.

## Symptom

In the proxy pane, unchanged CC content is colored GREEN (= "injected by the proxy") although the proxy never injected it. Concretely: a `user` `tool_result` block containing CC's own `<persisted-output>...</persisted-output>` marker is rendered green. Green must only mark content the proxy genuinely inserted; unchanged content must stay grey.

## Background (provided — do NOT go fetch this; no rag-cli / no web)

- Architecture axiom: on the MESSAGE level the proxy never *injects* — it only STRIPS, and a few strips REPLACE the stripped text with a small placeholder (e.g. `<task-notification>`→wakeup text, plan-mode reminder, bg-exit text, sidecar/idle-recap markers). Every such inject is small and is itself a consequence of a recorded strip.
- The intended span builder is `build_message_spans(orig_text, fwd_text, stripped_chunks)` in `src/proxy/diff_engine.py`: it splits `orig_text` at the positions of each recorded stripped chunk → equal/stripped segments, walks `fwd_text` matching the equal segments, and treats gaps in fwd between matched equal segments as `injected`. With an EMPTY chunk list it returns all-`equal` (no green at all).
- The call site is `_build_stripped_injected_deltas` in `src/proxy/logging.py`, message loop around line 322-327:
  `blk_chunks = [c for c in msg_chunks if c in o_inner]` then `if blk_chunks: spans, _ = build_message_spans(o_inner, f_inner, blk_chunks)`. When `blk_chunks` is empty, `spans` stays at its default `bd["spans"]`, which is the result of the word-level `_diff_text` (SequenceMatcher) path.
- `_diff_text`'s SequenceMatcher 'replace' opcode is the documented phantom source: it can emit the SAME text as both an equal/stripped span AND an injected span.
- `stripped_msg_removed` (keyed by message index) is computed in `apply_modification_rules` (`src/proxy/rules.py`), stashed on `flow.metadata["mc_stripped_msg_removed"]` in `addon.py` `request()` (~line 135), read back in `response()` (~line 233), and passed into `_build_stripped_injected_deltas`.

The live `_injected` log span sequence for the offending block (session 1780777036):
```

injected | '<persisted-output>\nOutput too large (36.6KB). Full output saved to: /Users/...'
equal    | '\n</persisted-output>'
```

i.e. the same content appears once as `equal` and once as `injected` (a duplicate).

If no chunk for this index, was the strip recorded under a different index, or not recorded? This is the crux.

**Was die Spans bedeuten.** Jeder Span ist getaggt als `equal`, `stripped` oder `injected`. Per Definition:
- `equal` = Text, der in **beiden** steht (orig und fwd), unverändert.
- `stripped` = Text, der nur in **orig** war (vom Proxy entfernt).
- `injected` = Text, der nur in **fwd** ist (vom Proxy hinzugefügt).

Daraus folgt, dass man aus den Spans beide Seiten wieder zusammenbauen kann:
- **orig** = alle `equal` + alle `stripped` Spans, der Reihe nach aneinandergehängt (orig enthielt ja alles: das Behaltene plus das Entfernte).
- **fwd** = alle `equal` + alle `injected` Spans, der Reihe nach (fwd enthält das Behaltene plus das Hinzugefügte).

**Der Round-Trip-Check.** Man baut beide Seiten aus den Spans nach und vergleicht byte-genau mit den echten Texten:
- `orig_ok` = (join der equal+stripped Spans) == echtes `orig_text`?
- `fwd_ok` = (join der equal+injected Spans) == echtes `fwd_text`?

Wenn beide stimmen, sind die Spans eine beweisbar verlustfreie, korrekte Beschreibung der Transformation. Wenn eine nicht stimmt, hat die Span-Berechnung etwas erzeugt, das mit der Realität nicht zusammenpasst — und dann sind auch die Farben falsch.

**Warum das genau unser Phantom fängt.** Im persisted-output-Fall waren die Spans `equal(open\n\n) + stripped(Preview…) + injected(open…) + equal(\n</persisted-output>)`. **In Produktion.** Nach dem Span-Bau (record-driven) läuft der Round-Trip. Bei `fwd_ok == False` (oder `orig_ok == False`) vertrauen wir der Overlay-Färbung für diesen Block nicht: wir rendern ihn neutral grau und loggen die Diskrepanz. Ein künftiger Recording-Gap oder eine Boundary-Verschiebung wird damit zu einem sichtbaren, aber **sicheren** „kein Overlay + Warnung" — statt zu einer stillen Falschfärbung. Der Source-Read hat einen **Fund ergeben, der den Scope verändert** — und der wichtiger ist als die reine Write-Entfernung. Ich gehe die Befunde von wichtig nach unwichtig durch.

**Die Read-Seite ist NICHT vollständig migriert — die Doku ist hier falsch.** Stage 2 hat nur die Proxy-Pane und die Warnings-Pane auf die Dual-Logs umgestellt. Es gibt aber einen **dritten, undokumentierten Consumer**: die **Haupt-Pane** (die Session-/Conversation-Ansicht). In `monitor.py:_refresh_strip_cache()` läuft noch `parse_proxy_log` über das Main-Log und speist einen Strip-Cache (`_strip_by_tool_id`). Dieser Cache treibt im Haupt-Display drei sichtbare Dinge: den **Pre-Strip-Volltext** von Tool-Outputs (du siehst, was CC ursprünglich bekam, bevor der Proxy gekürzt hat), das **Highlighting der gestrippten Stellen** im Tool-Output, und ein **Strip-Badge auf User-Prompts**. Quelle dafür sind die Main-Log-Felder `stripped_msg_indices` / `stripped_msg_originals` / `stripped_msg_removed`. Wenn wir das Main-Log einfach abschalten, **verliert die Haupt-Pane diese Strip-Anzeige** — ohne dass die Doku das erwähnt.

Das ist die eigentliche Entscheidung dieser Session: Entweder wir **migrieren auch diesen Consumer** auf die Dual-Logs (`_original` liefert den Pre-Strip-Volltext + die tool_use_id-Zuordnung, `_stripped` die entfernten Chunks) — das ist ein echtes Stück Parser-Arbeit analog zu Stage 2B, aber gut machbar — oder wir **verwerfen das Feature** in der Haupt-Pane, weil die Proxy-Pane die Strip/Inject-Daten ohnehin (sogar detaillierter) zeigt. Meine Empfehlung hängt davon ab, ob du diese Strip-Anzeige in der Haupt-Session-Ansicht tatsächlich nutzt. Wenn ja: migrieren. Wenn sie für dich redundant zur Proxy-Pane ist: streichen und damit die Migration deutlich verschlanken.

**Zwei gefährliche Kopplungen in der Rotation** (unabhängig von der obigen Entscheidung, müssen so oder so gefixt werden): Die Bash-Rotation in `claude_proxy_start.sh` leitet die zu behaltenden `log_id`s **aus den überlebenden Main-Logs** ab (Zeile 177).
